questions.explanation:
Production of cathode rays Cathode rays are produced in the discharge tube by applying the following conditions:

  • A high potential difference (>1200 V) is applied across the two aluminium electrodes.
  • The length of the tube is 30 cm and the diameter is 3 cm.
  • Pressure inside the tube is maintained below 0.01 mm of Hg.

questions.topicName: Discovery of Sub-Atomic Particles: Electron, Proton and Neutron
questions.explanation:
In 1909, Robert Millikan and Harvey Fletcher conducted the oil drop experiment to determine the charge of an electron. They suspended tiny charged droplets of oil between two metal electrodes by balancing downward gravitational force with upward drag and electric forces.

The experiment helped earn Millikan a Nobel prize in 1923

questions.topicName: Discovery of Sub-Atomic Particles: Electron, Proton and Neutron
questions.explanation:
Production of cathode rays: Cathode rays are produced in the discharge tube (by low pressure and high temperature) by applying the following conditions:

  • A high potential difference (>1200 V) is applied across the two aluminium electrodes.
  • The length of the tube is 30 cm and the diameter is 3 cm.
  • The pressure inside the tube is maintained below 0.01 mm of Hg.

questions.question: Television pictures result due to

options.option: fluorescence of canal rays on the television screen
fluorescence of cathode rays on the television screen
phosphorescence of protons on the television screen
fluorescence of protons on the television screen

questions.topicName: Discovery of Sub-Atomic Particles: Electron, Proton and Neutron
questions.explanation:
Cathode rays (also called an electron beam or e-beam) are streams of electrons observed in vacuum tubes. If an evacuated glass tube is equipped with two electrodes and a voltage is applied, the glass behind of the positive electrode is observed to glow, due to electrons emitted from and traveling away from the cathode (the electrode connected to the negative terminal of the voltage supply).
Cathode ray tubes (CRTs) use a focused beam of electrons deflected by electric or magnetic fields to create the image in a television set.

questions.question: Cathode rays are deflected by:
questions.topicName: Discovery of Sub-Atomic Particles: Electron, Proton and Neutron
questions.explanation: When an electric field is applied to a stream of cathode rays, they get deflected towards the positive plate. On the application of a magnetic field perpendicular to the path of the cathode rays, they get deflected in the direction expected of negative particles.

questions.question: The increasing order (lowest first) for the values of e/m (charge/mass) for electron (e ), proton (p), neutron (n) and alpha particle ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) is
questions.topicName: Discovery of Sub-Atomic Particles: Electron, Proton and Neutron
questions.explanation:
Neutron has no charge, hence elm is zero for neutron. Next, {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle (He2+) has very high mass compared to proton and electron, therefore very small elm ratio. Proton and electron have same charge (magnitude) but former is heavier, hence has smaller value of elm.
{tex}\frac{e}{m}{/tex} : n < {tex}\alpha{/tex} < p < e

questions.question: According to Dalton's atomic theory, the smallest particle in which matter can exist, is called
questions.topicName: Discovery of Sub-Atomic Particles: Electron, Proton and Neutron
questions.explanation:
DALTON'S ATOMIC THEORY :

  1. The matter is made up of very small indivisible particles called atoms.
  2. All the atoms of a given element identical in all respect i.e. mass, shape, size, etc.
  3. Atoms cannot be created nor destroyed by any chemical process.
  4. Atoms of different elements are different in nature.

options.option: Neutron in nucleus moves very fast

questions.topicName: Discovery of Sub-Atomic Particles: Electron, Proton and Neutron
questions.explanation:
Neutron in the free state immediately decomposes to proton and electron which makes its detection very difficult outside the nucleus.
{tex}_{0}^{1} n \rightarrow_{1}^{1} p+_{-1}^{0} e{/tex}

questions.question: If travelling at same speeds, which of the following matter waves have the shortest wavelength?
questions.topicName: Discovery of Sub-Atomic Particles: Electron, Proton and Neutron
questions.explanation:
{tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}=\frac{h}{p}{/tex}
The higher the mass, the shortest is the wavelength. since alpha particles have the highest mass (6.68*10-24g), they have the shortest wavelength.

questions.question: The increasing order (lowest first) for the values of e/m (charge/mass) for electron (e), proton (p), neutron (n) and alpha particle ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) is :
questions.topicName: Discovery of Sub-Atomic Particles: Electron, Proton and Neutron
questions.explanation:
{tex}\left(\frac{e}{m}\right)_{n}=\frac{0}{1.675 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}}=0{/tex}
{tex}\left(\frac{e}{m}\right)_{\propto}=\frac{2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}}{4 \times 1.675 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}}=0.5{/tex}
{tex}\left(\frac{e}{m}\right)_{p}=\frac{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}}{1.672 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}}=1{/tex}
{tex}\left(\frac{e}{m}\right)_{e}=\frac{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}}{9.108 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}}=1850{/tex}

questions.question: If proton and antiproton come close to each other and annihilate, how much energy will be released?
questions.topicName: Discovery of Sub-Atomic Particles: Electron, Proton and Neutron
questions.explanation:
When proton and antiproton annihilates, total energy released is
E = 2mc2
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 {tex}\times{/tex} (3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108)2
= 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 J

questions.question: If an electron and positron annihilate, then the energy released is:
questions.topicName: Discovery of Sub-Atomic Particles: Electron, Proton and Neutron
questions.explanation:
E = 2mc2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 {tex}\times{/tex} (3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108)2
= 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 J

questions.question:
If the Thomson model of the atom was correct, then the result of Rutherford’s gold foil experiment would have been:

  1. All of the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles pass through the gold foil without decrease in speed.
  2. {tex}\alpha{/tex}-Particles are deflected over a wide range of angles.
  3. All {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles get bounced back by 180o.
  4. {tex}\alpha{/tex}-Particles pass through the gold foil deflected by small angles and with reduced speed.
questions.topicName: Discovery of Sub-Atomic Particles: Electron, Proton and Neutron
questions.explanation: As in Thomson model, protons are diffused (charged is not centered) {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles deviate by small angles and due to repulsion from protons, their speed decreases.

questions.question: Which of the following is never true for cathode rays?
questions.topicName: Discovery of Sub-Atomic Particles: Electron, Proton and Neutron
questions.explanation: Cathode rays have kinetic energy, produces heat, can do work and produce mechanical pressure. They behave like negatively charged particles and do not behave as electromagnetic waves.

questions.explanation:
Mass number. The mass number (symbol A), also called atomic mass number or nucleon number, is the total number of protons and neutrons (together known as nucleons) in an atomic nucleus.
mass No. = no. of protons + no. of neutrons.

questions.question: Mg2+ is isoelectronic with:
questions.explanation:
Isoelectronic species refers to the elements that have the same number of electrons.
Mg2+ is 10 electron species {tex}(1s^2, 2s^2, 2p^6){/tex}. Its configuration is like that of Ne.
Thus it is isoelectronic with any element having 10e- or we can say 8e- in its valence shell.
Atomic number of Sodium (Na) is 11 after loosing one electron it became Na+ and have 10 electrons.
Thus Mg2+ is isoelectronic with Na+.

questions.question: How many neutrons and protons are there in the  {tex}^{13}_6C{/tex} nucleus?
questions.explanation:
Number of protons =  Atomic number =6
Number of neutrons = atomic mass - no of protons = 13-6 = 7

questions.question: An atom of an element contains 29 electrons and 35 neutrons. The number of protons are:
questions.explanation:
In an atom no. of protons = no. of electrons i.e. P = E while this is not true in case of ions.
So the number of protons in the given atom is 29.

questions.question: The atom with the given atomic number Z = 17, and the atomic mass A = 35.5 is
questions.explanation: Chlorine has atomic number 17 and atomic mass 35.5u.

questions.question: An element with mass number 81 contains 31.7 % more neutrons as compared to protons. Assign the atomic symbol.
questions.explanation:
P + N = 81
N = P + (0.317)P
Solving these two equations we get P = 35
The atomic number of Bromine is 35.
So the symbol is {tex}{}_{35}^{81}{\text{Br}}{/tex}

questions.question: If a cation B3+ has 18 electrons, the atomic number of element B will be
questions.explanation:
In the formation of a cation with 3+ charges, the atom loses 3 electrons.  
The no. of electrons in the atom will be 18 + 3 = 21.
In a neutral atom, the no. of protons is equal to the no. of electrons. So atomic no. is 21.

questions.question: In an atom of sodium with atomic number 11 and mass number 23,  the number of neutrons present is:

options.option: less than the number of protons
equal to the number of protons
greater than the number of protons
equal to the number of electrons

questions.explanation:
Atomic Number = Number of protons = 11
The number of neutrons = mass number - atomic number = 23-11= 12
Therefore, the number of neutrons i.e. 12 is greater than the number of protons i.e.11.

questions.question: The number of electrons in {tex}{\left[ {_{19}{K^{40}}} \right]^{ - 1}}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
The number of electrons equals the number of protons in a neutral atom. The positive charge equals the negative charge. The negative charge is the number of electrons. This ion has a charge of -1.
So, solve it for a negative charge.
−19 -1= -20
The negative charge is 20  so
e = 20

questions.question: The number of electrons and neutrons of an element is 18 and 20 respectively. Its mass number is
questions.explanation:
No. of proton and no. of electron =18 and No. of neutron = 20
Mass number = P + N =18 + 20 = 38

questions.question: Which of the following species is isotonic with {tex}_{37}R{b^{86}}{/tex}?
questions.explanation:
Isotonic species are those which have identical numbers of neutrons.
{tex}_{37} \mathrm{Rb}^{86} ; n=86-37=49{/tex}
{tex}_{38} \mathrm{Sr}^{87} ; n=87-38=49{/tex}
{tex}_{36} \mathrm{Kr}^{84} ; n=84-36=48\\ _{39} \mathrm{Y}^{89} ; \quad n=89-39=50\\ _{37} \mathrm{Mg}^{85} ; n=85-37=48 {/tex}

questions.question: {tex}S_{16}^{32}\,and\,P_{15}^{31}{/tex} can be correctly called as
questions.explanation:
Number of neutrons in carbon, NS= 32−16= 16
Number of neutrons in nitrogen, NP ​= 31−15 = 16
As both have the same number of neutrons, thus 32S16 and 31P15 are examples of isotones.

questions.question: If magnesium atom having atomic number 12 has an isotope with mass number 26, then the number of protons, neutrons and electrons respectively of the atom are
questions.explanation:
Isotopes are nuclei that have the same atomic number but different atomic mass.
∴ The atomic number of the isotope = 12
⇒ Number of electrons and protons = 12
The number of neutrons = At. mass - Atomic no.
= 26 - 12 = 14

questions.question: Two atoms are said to be isobars if:

options.option: they have the same number of electrons but different number of neutrons
they have the same number of neutrons but different number of electrons
the sum of the number of protons and neutrons is the same but the number of protons is different
they have the same atomic number but the different mass number

questions.explanation: Isobars have same number of nucleons but different no. of protons e.g. {tex}{ }_{18}^{40} \mathrm{Ar}{/tex} , {tex}{ }_{19}^{40} \mathrm{~K}{/tex}

questions.question: Chlorine exists in two isotopic forms, Cl-37 and Cl-35 but its atomic mass is 35.5. This indicates the ratio of Cl-37 and Cl-35 is approximate:
questions.explanation:
{tex}355=\frac{3.5 \times a+37 \times b}{a+b}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 3b or {tex}\frac{a}{b}{/tex} = 1 : 3

questions.question: The total number of fundamental particles in one atom of {tex}{ }_{6}^{14} \mathrm{C}{/tex} is:

questions.question: Atoms consist of electrons, protons and neutrons. If the mass attributed to neutron were halved and that attributed to the electrons were doubled, the atomic mass of 6C12 would be approximately:
questions.explanation: No change by doubling mass of electrons, however by reducing mass of neutron to half, total atomic mass becomes 6 + 3 instead of 6 + 6. Thus reduced by 25%.

questions.question: The total number of electrons in 1.6 g CH4 are:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\because{/tex} 16 g CH4 has N molecules and each molecule has 10 electrons
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1.6 g CH4 has {tex}\frac{N}{10} {/tex} molecules = {tex}\frac{N}{10} \times 10{/tex} electrons

questions.question: Atomic mass of Ne is 20.2. Ne is a mixture of Ne20 and Ne22. Relative abundance of heavier isotope is:
questions.explanation:
Average isotopic wt. = {tex}\Sigma \%{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} isotopic wt.
{tex}=\frac{(\text { per cent } \times \text { wt. of isotope })+(\text { per cent } \times \text { wt. of other isotope })}{100}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 20.2 = {tex}\frac{a \times 20+(100-a) \times 22}{100}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 90
per cent of lighter isotope = 100 - 90 = 10

questions.explanation: {tex}\mathrm{H}_{2}^{3} \mathrm{O}^{18}{/tex}

questions.question: If an isotope of hydrogen has two neutrons in its atom, its atomic number and mass number will be:

questions.question: Three isotopes of an element have mass numbers, M, (M + 1) and (M + 2). If the mean mass number is (M+ 0.5) then which of the following ratios may be accepted for M, (M + 1), (M + 2) in that order?
questions.explanation: Average mass = (M + 0.5) = {tex}\frac{M \times 4+(M+1) \times 1+(M+2) \times 1}{6}=\frac{6 M+3}{6}{/tex}

questions.question: The isoelectronic set of ions is

number of neutrons (n) = 122 - 51 = 71

71 = 51 + {tex}\frac{x}{100}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 51
x = 39.2%

The radii of the stationary states are expressed as : ${{\text{r}}_{\text{n}}} = {\text{ }}{{\text{n}}^{\text{2}}}{{\text{a}}_0}\left( {{\text{2}}.{\text{12}}} \right)$ where ${{\text{a}}_0} = {\text{ 52}}.{\text{9}}$ pm. questions.question: 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 atoms of carbon are arranged side by side. Calculate the radius of carbon atom if the length of this arrangement is 2.4 cm.
questions.explanation:
Total Length = 2.4 cm
Total number of atoms along the length = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 108
Therefore diameter of each atom = {tex}2.4 \over 2 × 10^8{/tex} = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 cm
And the radius of the atom = {tex} 1.2 \times 10^{-8}\over 2{/tex} = 0.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 cm = 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11m.

questions.question: Bohr’s model of atom explains the spectrum of:
questions.explanation:
The emission spectrum of atomic hydrogen is divided into a number of spectral series, with wavelengths given by the Rydberg formula. These observed spectral lines are due to the electron making transitions between two energy levels in the atom.
Bohr tells us that the electrons in the Hydrogen atom can only occupy discrete orbits around the nucleus (not at any distance from it but at certain specific, quantized, positions, or radial distances each one corresponding to an energetic state of your H atom) where they do not radiate energy.
When the electron moves from one allowed orbit to another it emits or absorbs photons of energy matching exactly the separation between the energies of the given orbits (emission/absorption spectrum).

questions.question: The energy associated with the first orbit in the hydrogen atom is -2.17 {tex}\times{/tex}10-18 J/atom. What is the energy associated with the fifth orbit?
questions.explanation:
The energy of first (Bohr) orbit in hydrogen atom = -2.17 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18Jatom-1
The energy of the fifth orbit will be given by {tex}E_n= E_1\times {Z^2\over N^2}{/tex}
 {tex}E_5={-2.17\times 10^{-18}\over 5^2}={/tex} 8.68 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-20Jatom-1

questions.question: Which transition in the hydrogen spectrum would have the same wavelength as the Balmer type transition from n = 4 to n = 2 of He+ spectrum
questions.explanation:
For He+ ion,
We have
{tex}{1\over λ }= Z^2R­_h{[{1\over n_1^2 }-{1\over n_2^2}] } {/tex}{tex}= 2^2R_h{ [{1\over 2^2} – {1\over 4^2}]}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{3}{4}{{R}_{h}}{/tex} ….(i)
Now for the hydrogen atom
{tex}{1\over λ }= R_h{ [{1\over n_1^2 }-{1\over n_2^2}] }{/tex}….(ii)
Equating equation (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}{1\over n_1^2} -{1\over n_2^2 }= {3\over 4}{/tex}
Obviously, n1 = 1 and n2 = 2
Hence, the transition n = 2 to n = 1 in hydrogen atom will have the same wavelength as the transition, n = 4 to n = 2 in He+ species.

questions.explanation:
The given statement is incorrect. For 1s-orbital radial probability density (R2) against r is given as:

For 1s-orbital, probability density decreases sharply as we move away from the nucleus.
The radial distribution curves obtained by plotting radial probability functions vs r for 1s-orbital is

questions.question: The ratio of the shortest wavelength of two spectral series of hydrogen spectrum is found to be about 9. The spectral series are
questions.explanation:
According to Rydberg’s equation,
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=\frac{R_{\mathrm{H}}}{h c}\left(\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right) \quad \text { or } \quad \frac{1}{\lambda} \propto\left(\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right){/tex}
For shortest wavelength, i.e. highest energy spectral line, n2 will be ({tex}\infty{/tex}).
For the given spectral series, ratio of the shortest wavelength of two spectral series can be calculated as follows :

  1. {tex}\frac{\lambda_{L}}{\lambda_{P}}=\frac{\frac{1}{3^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}}{\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}}=\frac{\frac{1}{9}-0}{1-0}=\frac{1}{9}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{Bk}}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{Pf}}}=\frac{\frac{1}{5^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}}{\frac{1}{4^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}}=\frac{1}{25} \times \frac{16}{1}=\frac{16}{25}{/tex}
  3. {tex}\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{P}}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{Pf}}}=\frac{\frac{1}{5^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}}{\frac{1}{3^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}}=\frac{1}{25} \times \frac{9}{1}=\frac{9}{25}{/tex}
  4. {tex}\frac{\lambda_{B}}{\lambda_{B k}}=\frac{\frac{1}{4^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}}{\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}}=\frac{1}{16} \times \frac{4}{1}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: For any given series of spectral lines of atomic hydrogen, let {tex}\Delta ar{ u}= ar{ u}_{\max }- ar{ u}_{\min }{/tex} be the difference in maximum and minimum frequencies in cm-1. The ratio {tex} rac{{\Delta {{ ar u}_{{ ext{Lyman}}}}}}{{\Delta {{ ar u}_{{ ext{Balmer}}}}}}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
For any given series of spectral lines of atomic hydrogen.
Let {tex}\Delta \bar{\nu}=\bar{\nu}_{\max }-\bar{\nu}_{\min }{/tex} be the difference in maximum and minimum frequencies in cm-1.
For Lyman series, {tex}\Delta \bar{\nu}=\bar{\nu}_{\max }-\bar{\nu}_{\min }{/tex}
General formula: {tex}\bar{v}=109677\left[\frac{1}{n_{i}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{f}^{2}}\right]{/tex}
For Lyman n1 = 1, n2 = 2, 3, ...
{tex}\bar{v}_{\max }=109,677\left(\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right)=109,677\left(\frac{1}{1}-0\right){/tex} = 109.677
{tex}\bar{v}_{\min }=109,677\left(\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{(2)^{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\begin{aligned} \Delta \bar{v}_{\text {Lyman }} &=\bar{v}_{\max }-\bar{v}_{\min } \\ \end{aligned}{/tex}
= {tex}=109,677-\left[\frac{109,677 \times 3}{4}\right]=\frac{109,677}{4}{/tex}
For Balmer series,
{tex}\bar{v}_{\max }=109,677\left(\frac{1}{(2)^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right) \Rightarrow \frac{109677}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\bar{v}_{\min }=109,677\left(\frac{1}{(2)^{2}}-\frac{1}{(3)^{2}}\right) \Rightarrow \frac{109677 \times 5}{36}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta \bar{v} =\bar{v}_{\max }-\bar{v}_{\min }{/tex}
{tex}\begin{aligned} \Delta \bar{v}_{\text {Balmer }} &=\frac{109,677}{4}-\left[\frac{109,677}{36} \times 5\right] \end{aligned}{/tex} = {tex}109,677\left(\frac{1}{9}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{\Delta \bar{v}_{\mathrm{Lyman}}}{\Delta \bar{v}_{\mathrm{Balmer}}}=\frac{109,677 / 4}{109,677 / 9}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\Delta \bar{v}_{\mathrm{Lyman}}}{\Delta \bar{v}_{\mathrm{Balmer}}}=\frac{9}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The ratio of {tex}\frac{\Delta \bar{v}_{\mathrm{Lyman}}}{\Delta \bar{v}_{\mathrm{Balmer}}} \text { is } 9: 4{/tex}

questions.question:
Which of the following combination of statements is true regarding the interpretation of the atomic orbitals?

  1. An electron in an orbital of high angular momentum stays away from the nucleus than an electron in the orbital of lower angular momentum.
  2. For a given value of the principal quantum number, the size of the orbit is inversely proportional to the azimuthal quantum number.
  3. According to wave mechanics, the ground state angular momentum is equal to {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex}.
  4. The plot of {tex}\psi{/tex} vs r for various azimuthal quantum numbers, shows peak shifting towards higher r value.
questions.explanation:
1. Angular momentum, {tex}m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} mvr {tex}\propto{/tex} n
    {tex}\propto{/tex} distance from the nucleus
  2. This statement is incorrect as size of an orbit {tex}\propto{/tex} Azimuthal quantum number (l) ({tex}\because{/tex} n = constant)
  3. This statement is incorrect as at ground state,
    n = 1, l = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Orbital angular momentum (wave mechanics)
    {tex}\begin{aligned} =\sqrt{l(l+1)} \frac{h}{2 \pi}=0 \ [\because l=0] \end{aligned}{/tex}
  4. The given plot is

questions.question:
Heat treatment of muscular pain involves radiation of wavelength of about 900 nm. Which spectral line of H-atom is suitable for this purpose?
[RH = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 cm-1, h = 6.6{tex}\times{/tex} 1034 Js, c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 ms-1]
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta E=h c \times \frac{1}{\lambda}=h c \times\left[R_{\mathrm{H}}\left(\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right) \times Z^{2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}=\frac{h c}{R_{\mathrm{H}} \times \lambda \times Z^{2} \times h c}{/tex} [for H, atom Z = 1]
{tex}=\frac{1}{R_{H} \times \lambda}=\frac{1}{\left(1 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{m}^{-1}\right)} \times \frac{1}{\left(900 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}=\frac{1}{9}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{3^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}=\frac{1}{9}-0=\frac{1}{9}{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} {\therefore n_{1}=3} \\ {n_{2}=\infty} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

questions.question: For emission line of atomic hydrogen from ni =8 to nf = n, the plot of wave number ({tex}\nu{/tex}) against ({tex}\frac{1}{n^2}{/tex}) will be (The Rydberg constant, RH is in wave number unit)
questions.explanation:
According to Rydberg ’ s formula,
wave number (v) = {tex}R_{\mathrm{H}} Z^{2}\left[\frac{1}{n_{i}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{f}^{2}}\right]{/tex}
Given, ni = n, nf = 8 [{tex}\because{/tex} it is the case of emission]
{tex}\bar{v}=R_{H} \times(1)^{2}\left[\frac{1}{n^{2}}-\frac{1}{8^{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\bar{v}=R_{H}\left[\frac{1}{n^{2}}-\frac{1}{64}\right]=\frac{R_{H}}{n^{2}}-\frac{R_{H}}{64}{/tex}
On comparing with equation of straight line, y = mx + c, we get
Slope = RH, intercept = {tex}\frac{-R_{\mathrm{H}}}{64}{/tex}
Thus, plot of wave number ({tex}\overline v{/tex}) against {tex}\frac{1}{n^{2}}{/tex} will be linear with slope (+RH).

questions.question: The radius of the second Bohr orbit for hydrogen atom is (Planck’s constant (h ) = 6.6262 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 Js; mass of electron = 9.1091 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg ; charge of electron (e) = 1.60210 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C; permitivity of vacuum({tex}\varepsilon_o{/tex}) =  8.854185 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 kg-1m-3A2)
questions.explanation:
Bohr radius (rn) = {tex}\in_{0} {/tex}n2h2
rn = {tex}\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m e^{2} k Z}{/tex}
k = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} rn = {tex}\frac{n^{2} h^{2} \epsilon_{0}}{\pi m e^{2} Z}{/tex} = n2{tex}\frac{a_{0}}{Z}{/tex}
where, m = mass of electron
e = charge of electron
h = Planck’s constant
k = Coulomb constant
{tex}r_{n}=\frac{n^{2} \times 0.53}{z} \mathrm{\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} }{/tex}
Radius of nth Bohr orbit for H-atom
= 0.53 n2 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex} [Z = 1 for H-atom]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius of 2nd Bohr orbit for H-atom
= 0.53 {tex}\times{/tex} (2)2 = 2.12 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex}

questions.question: P is the probability of finding the 1s electron of a hydrogen atom in a spherical shell of infinitesimal thickness, dr, at a distance r from the nucleus. The volume of this shell is 4{tex}\pi{/tex}r2dr. The qualitative sketch of the dependence of P on r is
questions.explanation:
This graph shows the probability of finding the electron within the shell at various distances from the nucleus (radial probability). The curve shows the maximum, which means that the radial probability is greatest for a given distance from the nucleus. This distance is equal to Bohr’s radius = a0

  1. It is for 2s-orbital.
  2. It is radial wave function for Is.
  3. Correct
  4. Probability cannot be zero at a certain distance from nucleus

questions.question: Which of the following is the energy of a possible excited state of hydrogen?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\therefore \ E_{n}=-\frac{13.6}{n^{2}} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} where, n = 1, 2, 3 ...
In excited states, {tex}E_{2}=\frac{-13.6}{4}{/tex} = -3.4 eV

questions.question:
Energy of an electron is given by 
{tex}E=-2.178 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{J}\left(\frac{Z^{2}}{n^{2}}\right){/tex} 
Wavelength of light required to excite an electron in an hydrogen atom from level n = 1 to n = 2 will be
(h = 6.62 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 Js and c = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 ms-1)
questions.explanation:
Given, in the question E = -2.178 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 J {tex}\left[\frac{Z^{2}}{n^{2}}\right]{/tex}
For hydrogen Z = 1,
So, E1 = -2.178 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 J {tex}\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}\right]{/tex}
E2 = -2.178 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 J {tex}\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right]{/tex}
Now, E1 - E2
i.e. {tex}\Delta E=2.178 \times 10^{-18}\left(\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right)=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}2.178 \times 10^{-18}\left(\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right)=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34} \times 3.0 \times 10^{8}}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda \approx{/tex} 1.21 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m

questions.question: The kinetic energy of an electron in the second Bohr orbit of a hydrogen atom is [a0 is Bohr radius]
questions.explanation:
According to Bohr’s model,
{tex}m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi} \Rightarrow(m v)^{2}=\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} r^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{8 \pi^{2} r^{2} m}{/tex} ...(i)
Also, Bohr’s radius for H-atom is, r - n2a0
Substituting 'r' in Eq. (i) gives
KE = {tex}\frac{h^{2}}{8 \pi^{2} n^{2} a_{0}^{2} m}{/tex} when n = 2, KE = {tex}\frac{h^{2}}{32 \pi^{2} a_{0}^{2} m}{/tex}

questions.question: Which hydrogen like species will have same radius as that of Bohr orbit of hydrogen atom?
questions.explanation: Expression for Bohr’s orbit is, {tex}r_{n}=\frac{a_{0} n^{2}}{Z}=a_{0}{/tex} when n = 2, Z = 4

questions.question: The energy of an electron in the first Bohr orbit of H-atom is -13.6 eV. The possible energy value(s) of the excited state(s) for electrons in Bohr orbits of hydrogen is (are)
questions.explanation:
The energy of an electron in a Bohr atom is expressed as
{tex}E_{n}=-\frac{k Z^{2}}{n^{2}}{/tex} where, k = Constant, Z = Atomic number, n = Orbit number
= -13.6 eV for H(n = 1)
when n = 2, {tex}E_{2}=\frac{-13.6}{2^{2}}{/tex} eV = -3.40 eV
(n can have only integral value 1, 2, 3,.......{tex}\infty{/tex})

questions.question: For a d-electron, the orbital angular momentum is
questions.explanation:
The orbital angular momentum (L) = {tex}\sqrt{l(l+1)} \frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
{tex}\sqrt{6}\left(\frac{h}{2 \pi}\right){/tex} (l = 2 for d-orbital)

questions.question: The orbital angular momentum of an electron in 2s-orbital is
questions.explanation:
Expression for orbital angular momentum (L) is
{tex}L=\sqrt{l(l+1)} \frac{h}{2 \pi}=0{/tex} for 2 s-electrons
{tex}\therefore{/tex} For s-orbital, l = 0

questions.question: The wavelength of a spectral line for an electronic transition is inversely related to

options.option: the nuclear charge of the atom
the difference in the energy of the energy levels involved in the transition
the velocity of the electron undergoing the transition
the number of electrons undergoing the transition

questions.explanation:
Transition energy {tex}(\Delta E)=k Z^{2}\left(\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right)=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex}
i.e. {tex}\Delta E \propto \frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex}

questions.question: The ratio of the energy of a photon of 2000 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex} wavelength radiation to that of 4000 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex} radiation is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex}; {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} = 2000 {tex}\overset oA{/tex}; {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} = 4000{tex}\overset oA{/tex};
so {tex}\frac{E_{1}}{E_{2}}=\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {4000}{2000}{/tex} = 2

questions.question: The maximum energy is possessed by an electron, when it is present

options.option: At infinite distance from the nucleus

questions.explanation: As the electron goes away from the nucleus its energy increases sharply.

questions.question: Spectrum of Li2+ is similar to that of
questions.explanation: One of the lines in the emission spectrum of Li2+ has the same wavelength as that of the second line of the Balmer series in the hydrogen spectrum.

questions.question: We can say that the energy of a photon of frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} is given by E = h{tex}\nu{/tex}, where h is Planck’s constant. The momentum of a photon is p = {tex}\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} , where {tex}\lambda{/tex} is the wavelength of photon. Then we may conclude that velocity of light is equal to:
questions.explanation:
{tex}p=m u=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} and {tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}E=\frac{c}{\lambda} \cdot p \lambda=c . p{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} c = {tex}\frac{E}{p}{/tex}

questions.question: The energy of electromagnetic radiation depends on:

options.option: temperature of medium through which it passes

questions.explanation: E = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex}

questions.question: The angular momentum of electron of H-atom is proportional to:
questions.explanation:
From Bohr’s concept {tex}\frac{m u^{2}}{r}=\frac{e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{m r^{2} \cdot m u^{2}}{r}=\frac{e^{2} m r^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex}
or (angular momentum)2 = e2mr: angular momentum {tex}\propto{/tex} {tex}\sqrt r{/tex}

questions.question: According to Bohr’ postulates which quantity can take up only discrete values:
questions.explanation: Angular momentum = {tex}\text { n. } \frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex}, where n is integer and thus discrete value.

questions.question: The ratio of the difference in energy of electron between the first and second Bohr’s orbits to that between second and third Bohr’s orbits is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}E_{n}=\frac{E_{1}}{n^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{E_{1}-\frac{E_{1}}{4}}{\frac{E_{1}}{4}-\frac{E_{1}}{9}}=\frac{3 E_{1}}{4} \times \frac{36}{5 E_{1}}=\frac{27}{5}{/tex}

questions.question: If each hydrogen atom is excited by giving 8.4 eV of energy, then the number of spectral lines emitted is equal to:
questions.explanation:
For excitation of an electron from the ground state the minimum energy needed is 10.2 eV;
E2 - E1 = -3.4 - (-13.6)

questions.question: The number of degenerate orbitals in a level of H-atom having energy {tex}E_{n}=-\frac{R_{H}}{9}{/tex}.
questions.explanation:
{tex}E_{n}=\frac{R_{H}}{9}=\frac{R_{H}}{n^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} n = 3
Thus it is 3rd shell i.e., having 3s, 3p and 3d orbitals degenerate orbitals means orbitals having same energy levels and thus one of s and 3 of p and 5 of d orbitals have same energy levels.

questions.question: The electronic velocity in the fourth Bohr’s orbit of hydrogen is u. The velocity of the electron in the first orbit would be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}u_{n}=\frac{u_{1}}{n}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}u=\frac{u_{1}}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} u1 = 4u

questions.question: The amount of energy required to remove the electron from a Li2+ ion in its ground state is how many times greater than the amount of energy required to remove the electron from an H atom in its ground state:
questions.explanation:
{tex}E_{\mathrm{Li}^{2+}}=E_{\mathrm{H}} \times Z^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{E_{1 \mathrm{Li}^{2+}}}{E_{1 \mathrm{H}}}{/tex} = Z2 = 32 = 9

questions.question: In an atom two electrons move around the nucleus in circular orbits of radii R and 4R. The ratio of the time taken by them to complete one revolution is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}T=\frac{2 \pi r_{n}}{u_{n}}=\frac{2 \pi r_{1} \times n^{2}}{u_{1} / n}{/tex}
Also {tex}r_{n}=n^{2} \times r_{1}{/tex} and {tex}u_{n}=\frac{u_{1}}{n}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{4 R}{R}=n^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} n = 2
or {tex}T \propto n^{3}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=\frac{1^{3}}{2^{3}}=\frac{1}{8}{/tex}

questions.question: If the series limit of wavelength of the Lyman series for the hydrogen atom is 912 {tex}\overset oA{/tex}, then the series limit of wavelength for the Balmer series of the hydrogen atom is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {Lyman }}}=R_{\mathrm{H}}\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right]=R_{H}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {Balmer }}}=R_{\mathrm{H}}\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right]=\frac{R_{H}}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\lambda_{\text {Balmer }}}{\lambda_{\text {Lyman }}}=4 \text { or } \lambda_{B}=4 \times 912 \mathop A\limits^o {/tex}

questions.question: The difference in angular momentum associated with the electron in two successive orbits of hydrogen atom is:
questions.explanation:
Angular momentum for n and (n + 1) shells are {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} and {tex}(n+1) \frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
Thus, difference in angular momentum of two successive orbits is {tex}(n+1) \frac{h}{2 \pi}-\frac{n h}{2 \pi}=\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex}

questions.question: Ionisation potential of hydrogen atom is 13.6 eV. Hydrogen atom in the ground state are excited by monochromatic light of energy 12.1 eV. The spectral lines emitted by hydrogen according to Bohr's theory will be:
questions.explanation:
The electron in H atom is excited to III shell after absorbing 12.1 e V; because,
{tex}E_{3}-E_{1}=\frac{-13.6}{9}+13.6{/tex} = 12.1
Thus possible transitions from 3rd to I shell are {tex}\Sigma{/tex}({tex}\Delta n{/tex}) = {tex}\Sigma{/tex} (3 - 1) = {tex}\Sigma{/tex}2 = 1 + 2 = 3.

questions.question: One requires energy En to remove a nucleon and an energy Ee to remove an electron from the orbit of an atom, then:
questions.explanation: En > Ee

questions.question: The shortest {tex}\lambda{/tex} for the Lyman series is ________. (Given RH = 109678cm-1)
questions.explanation:
When {tex}\Delta E=\frac{h c}{\lambda} {/tex} is maximum then {tex}\lambda{/tex} is smallest
Thus, {tex}\Delta E=E_{\infty}-E_{1}{/tex}
For shortest {tex}\lambda{/tex} of Lyman series,
n1 = 1 and n2 = {tex}\infty{/tex}; {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R_{\mathrm{H}}\left[\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right]{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R_{H}{/tex} = 109678
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda=911 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~cm}=911 \mathop A\limits^o {/tex}

questions.question: The longest {tex}\lambda{/tex} for the Lyman series is ________. (Given RH = 109678cm-1)
questions.explanation:
For longest {tex}\lambda{/tex} of Balmer series n1 = 1 and n2 = 2
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R_{\mathrm{H}}\left[\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right]{/tex}
because {tex}\Delta E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex} is minimum when {tex}\lambda{/tex} is longest
Thus, {tex}\Delta E=E_{2}-E_{1}{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R_{H} \times\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right]=\frac{3}{4} \times 109678{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda=1.215 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~cm}=1215 \mathop A\limits^o {/tex}

questions.question: What transition in He+ ion shall have the same wavenumber as the first line in the Balmer series of a hydrogen atoms?
questions.explanation:
{tex}E_{3}-E_{2}{/tex} for {tex}\mathrm{H}=\frac{E_{1 \mathrm{H}}}{9}+\frac{E_{1 \mathrm{H}}}{4}=\frac{5 E_{\mathrm{IH}}}{36}{/tex}{tex}\left[\because E_{n \mathrm{H}}=-\frac{E_{1 \mathrm{H}}}{n^{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}E_{6}-E_{4}{/tex} for {tex}\mathrm{He}^{+}=\left[-\frac{E_{1 \mathrm{H}} \times 2^{2}}{6^{2}}+\frac{E_{1 \mathrm{H}} \times 2^{2}}{4^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[E_{n \mathrm{He}^{+}}=-\frac{E_{1 \mathrm{H}} \times Z^{2}}{n^{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5 E_{1 \mathrm{H}}}{36}{/tex}

questions.question: If Un is the speed of electron in n shell, which of the following is independent of (Un {tex}\times{/tex} n)?
questions.explanation:
{tex}u_{n}=\frac{u_{1}}{n}{/tex}
{tex}u_{n} \times n=\frac{u_{1}}{n} \times n=u_{1}{/tex} i.e., independent of n

questions.question: The mass of electron moving with velocity of light is:
questions.explanation: {tex}m_{e}^{\prime}=\frac{m_{e}}{\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{u}{c}\right)^{2}}}{/tex}

questions.question: Splitting of spectral lines when atoms are subjected to strong electric field is called:
questions.explanation: Stark effect

questions.question: The potential energy of the electron present in the ground state of Li2+ ion is represented by:
questions.explanation: P.E. = {tex}\frac{-Z e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} (Z = 3 for Li2+)

questions.question: The energy levels for {tex}Z^{A^{+(z-1)}}{/tex} can be given by:
questions.explanation: {tex}E_{\mathrm{He}^{+}}=E_{\mathrm{H}} \times 2^{2} ; E_{\mathrm{Li}^{2+}}=E_{\mathrm{H}} \times 3^{2}{/tex}

questions.question: For an electron in a hydrogen atom, the wave function, {tex}\psi{/tex} is proportional to exp - ({tex}\frac{r}{a_{0}}{/tex}), where a0 is the Bohr’s radius. What is the ratio of the probability of finding the electron at the nucleus to the probability of finding it at a0?
questions.explanation:
a0 = 0, if electron is in nucleus.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}e^{-r / a_{0}}=0{/tex}

questions.question: The difference in Bohr’s radius of H atom for (n + 1) and nth radius is equal to (n - 1) Bohr radius. The value of n is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}r_{(n+1)}-r_{n}=r_{n-1}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}r \propto n^{2}{/tex}
(n+1)2 - n2 = (n - 1)2
n2 + 1 + 2n - n2 = n2 + 1 - 2n
n2 - 4n = 0
{tex}\therefore {/tex} n = 4

questions.question: The approximate quantum number of a circular orbit of diameter, 10.3 nm of the hydrogen atom according to Bohr’s theory is:
questions.explanation:
Given rn = 10.3 nm
{tex}r_{n}=r_{1} \times n^{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}n^{2}=\frac{r_{n}}{r_{1}}=\frac{10.3 \times 10^{-9}}{0.529 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} n = 14

questions.question: The ratio of kinetic energy and potential energy of an electron in any orbit is equal to:
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{K E}{P E}=\frac{\frac{e^2}{2 r}}{\frac{c^2}{r}}=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The angular momentum of an electron in 2p orbital is:
questions.explanation:
Angular momentum in an orbital
{tex}=\frac{h}{2 \pi} \sqrt{l(l+1)}=\frac{h}{2 \pi} \times \sqrt{2}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2} \pi}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} l = 1)

questions.question: The ratio of the energy of the electron in ground state of hydrogen to the electron in first excited state of Be3+ is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}E_{2\left(\mathrm{Be}^{3+}\right)}=E_{2(\mathrm{H})} \times Z^{2}{/tex}; Also {tex}E_{2(\mathrm{H})}=\frac{E_{1(\mathrm{H})}}{2^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}E_{2\left(\mathrm{Be}^{3+}\right)}=\frac{E_{1(\mathrm{H})}}{2^{2}} \times 4^{2}=4 \times E_{1(\mathrm{H})}{/tex}

questions.question: If the shortest wavelength of H atom in Lyman series is x, then longest wavelength in Balmer series of He+ is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{L}}=R_{H}\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right]{/tex} for shortest {tex}\lambda{/tex} of Lyman series, of H.
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{B}}=Z^{2} R_{H}\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right]{/tex} for longest {tex}\lambda{/tex}, of Balmer series of He+. 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_{B}}{\lambda_{A}}=\frac{1 \times 36}{5 \times 4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{B}=\frac{9}{5} {/tex}, {tex} \lambda_{L}=\frac{9}{5} x{/tex}

questions.question: The electronic transition from n = 2 to n = 1 will produce shortest wavelength in:
questions.explanation: {tex}\Delta E{/tex} for Li2+ = {tex}E_{2}-E_{1}{/tex} is maximum as {tex}E_{n \mathrm{Li}^{2+}}=E_{n \mathrm{H}} \times Z^{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The first emission line of Balmer series in H spectrum has the wave number equal to:
questions.explanation: {tex}\bar{v}=\frac{1}{\lambda}=R_{\mathrm{H}}\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right]{/tex}; n1 = 2 for Balmer series; n2 = 3 for {tex}\mathrm{H}_{\alpha}{/tex} line.

questions.question: When greater number of excited hydrogen atoms reach the ground state:

options.option: Both the intensity and number of lines in Lyman series increases
More number of lines are found in Lyman series
The intensity of lines in Balmer series increases
The intensity of lines in Lyman series increases

questions.explanation: Intensity, i.e., no. of photons emitted out per sec. increases.

questions.question: For which of the following process energy is absorbed?

options.option: separating an electron from a proton
separating an electron from an electron
Separating a proton from a proton
Adding an Electron into a neutral atom

questions.explanation: Energy is needed to overpower the forces of attractions among oppositely charged particles.

ratom =  ........ {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 cm

questions.question: The wavelength of a spectral line for an electronic transition is inversely related to:

options.option: the velocity of the electron undergoing the transition
the number of electrons undergoing the transition
the nuclear charge of the atom
the difference in the energy of the energy levels involved in the transition

questions.explanation: {tex}\Delta E=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex} or {tex}\Delta E \propto \frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex}

questions.question: The wave no. for the shortest wave length transition in the Balmer series of atomic hydrogen is:
questions.explanation:
For shortest wavelength {tex}\Delta E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex}, {tex}\Delta E{/tex} should be maximum. Thus n1 = 2 and n2 = {tex}\infty{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\bar{v}=\frac{1}{\lambda}=R_{H}\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right]=109677 \times \frac{1}{4^{2}}{/tex}
= 27419.5 cm-1

questions.question: If the speed of electron in the Bohr’s first orbit of hydrogen atom is x, the speed of the electron in the third Bohr’s orbit is:
questions.explanation: {tex}u_n=\frac{u}{n}{/tex}

questions.question: A hydrogen atom in its ground state absorbs a photon. The maximum energy of such a photon is:
questions.explanation: E1 = -13.6 eV; Thus, it can absorb 13.6 eV to get itself knocked out.

questions.question: Which electron transition in a hydrogen atom requires the largest amount of energy?
questions.explanation: From n = 1 to n = 2

questions.question: The radius of second stationary orbit in Bohr’s atoms is R. The radius of third orbit will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}r_{n}=r_{1} \times n^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{r_{3}}{r_{2}}=\frac{9}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: The radius of Bohr’s first orbit in H-atom is 0.053 nm. The radius of second orbit in He+ would be:
questions.explanation:
rn for He+ = {tex}\frac{r_{n} \text { for } \mathrm{H}}{Z}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} r2 for He+ = {tex}\frac{r_{2} \text { for } \mathrm{H}}{2}=\frac{r_{1} \text { for } \mathrm{H} \times 2^{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\left(\because r_{n}=r_{1} \times n^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} r2 for He+ = 0.053 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 0.106 nm

questions.question: The highest excited state that unexcited hydrogen atom can reach when they are bombarded with 12.2 eV electron is:
questions.explanation:
E1 = -13.6 eV
After providing 12.2 eV energy
EH = −13.6 + 12.2
= 1.4 eV
Now {tex}E_{n}=\frac{E_{1}}{n^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}n^{2}=\frac{-13.6}{-1.4}{/tex} = 9.71
{tex}\therefore{/tex} n {tex}\approx{/tex} 3 (Actually 12.09 eV energy is absorbed to attain 3rd energy level)

questions.question: The angular momentum of electron in nth orbit is given by:
questions.explanation: Angular momentum of electron in an orbit = {tex}n \frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex}

questions.question: E1 for He+ is -54.4 eV. The E2 for He+ would be:
questions.explanation:
En = {tex}\frac{E_1}{n^2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} E2 = {tex}\frac{-54.4}{4}{/tex} = -13.6eV

questions.question: If the radius of first Bohr’s orbit be a a0, then the radius of third Bohr’s orbit would be:
questions.explanation: rn = r1 {tex}\times{/tex} n2

questions.question: The energy of electron in first orbit of He+ is(RH = -871.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-20J). The energy of electron in the first orbit of H is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}E_{1 \mathrm{He}^{+}}=E_{1 \mathrm{H}} \times Z^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}-871.6 \times 10^{-20}=E_{1 \mathrm{H}} \times 4{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}E_{1 \mathrm{H}}=-217.9 \times 10^{-20} \mathrm{~J}{/tex}

questions.question: The energy of an electron in the first Bohr’s orbit of H-atom is -13.6 eV. The possible energy value (s) of the excited state (s) for electrons in Bohr’s orbits of hydrogen is (are):
questions.explanation:
E1 for H = -13.6eV; En for H = {tex}\frac{E_{1}}{n^{2}}{/tex};
Thus, {tex}E_{2}=\frac{-13.6}{4}=-3.4 \mathrm{eV}{/tex}; {tex}E_{3}=\frac{-13.6}{9}=-1.51{/tex};
{tex}E_{4}=\frac{-13.6}{16}{/tex} = 0.85 and so on.

questions.question: The wavelength of radiation emitted when electron falls from 4th Bohr’s orbit to 2nd in H-atom is:
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R_{\mathrm{H}} \times\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right]{/tex} = 4.86 {tex}\times {/tex} 10-7 = 486 nm

questions.question: In a hydrogen atom, if energy of an electron in ground state is -13.6 eV, then that in the 2nd excited state is:
questions.explanation:
2nd excited state means 3rd energy level.
{tex}E_{3}=\frac{E_{1}}{n^{2}}=\frac{-13.6}{9}=-1.51 \mathrm{eV}{/tex}

questions.question: In Bohr series of lines of hydrogen spectrum, the third line from the red end corresponds to which one of the following inter-orbit jumps of the electron for Bohr’s orbits in an atom of hydrogen?
questions.explanation: Visible series for Balmer and third line from 5 tends to 2.

questions.question: For which of the following, the radius will be same as for hydrogen atom having n =1?
questions.explanation: {tex}r_{2} \mathrm{Be}^{3+}=\frac{r_{1} \mathrm{H}}{4} \times 2^{2}{/tex} ({tex}\because r_{2} \mathrm{H}=r_{1 \mathrm{H}} \times 2^{2}{/tex} and {tex}r_{n} \mathrm{Be}^{3+}=\frac{r_{n} \mathrm{H}}{n}{/tex})

questions.question: The wavelengths of the radiations emitted when in a H atom, electron falls from infinity to stationary state 1, is:
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R_{\mathrm{H}}\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right]{/tex}

questions.question: According to Bohr’s theory, the angular momentum of an electron in 5th orbit is:
questions.explanation: Angular momentum = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}=\frac{5 h}{2 \pi}=2.5 \frac{h}{\pi}{/tex} (n = 5)

questions.question: The ionisation enthalpy of hydrogen atom is 1.312 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 J mol-1. The energy required to excite the electron in the atom from n = 1 to n = 2 is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta E=E_{2}-E_{1}=-\frac{1.312 \times 10^{6}}{4}+1.312 \times 10^{6}{/tex}
9.84 {tex}\times{/tex} 105Jmol-1

questions.question: Ionisation energy of He+ is 19.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 J atom-1. The energy of the first stationary state (n = 1) of Li2+ is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}E_{\mathrm{He}^{+}}=E_{\mathrm{IH}} \times Z_{\mathrm{1}}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}E_{\mathrm{Li}^{2+}}=E_{1 \mathrm{H}} \times Z_{2}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}E_{\mathrm{Li}^{2+}}=E_{\mathrm{He}^{+}} \times \frac{Z_{2}^{2}}{Z_{1}^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{19.6 \times 10^{-18} \times 3^{2}}{2^{2}}{/tex}
= 4.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17J atom-1
Energy in orbit = {tex}-E_{\mathrm{Li}^{2+}}{/tex}

questions.question:
The energy required to break one mole of Cl-Cl bonds in Cl2 is 242 kJ mol-1. The longest wavelength of light capable of breaking single Cl—Cl bond is:
(c = 3 {tex}\times {/tex} 108ms-1 and NA =6.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 mol-1)
questions.explanation:
{tex}E=\frac{N \cdot h c}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{6.023 \times 10^{23} \times 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3.0 \times 10^{8}}{242 \times 10^{3}}{/tex}
= 4.946 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m = 494.6 nm

questions.question: The kinetic energy of an electron in the second Bohr’s orbit of a hydrogen atom is : (a0 is Bohr’s radius)
questions.explanation:
As per Bohr’s postulate, kinetic energy in II orbit
{tex}=+\frac{e^{2}}{2 r_{2}}=\frac{e^{2}}{2 a_{0} \times 2^{2}} \quad\left(\because r_{2}=r_{1} \times n^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{e^{2}}{8 a_{0}}{/tex}
Since, {tex}a_{0}=\frac{h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m e^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Kinetic energy in II orbit = {tex}\frac{h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m a_{0}} \times \frac{1}{8 a_{0}}=\frac{h^{2}}{32 \pi^{2} m a_{0}^{2}}{/tex}

questions.question: A stream of electrons from a heated filament was passed between two charged plates kept at a potential difference V esu. If eand m are charge and mass of an then the value of {tex}\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex}(where {tex}\lambda{/tex} is wavelength associated with electron wave is give by:
questions.explanation:
The unit of potential difference is given as esu however it has no significance as other units are not reported. Also change in kinetic energy of electron is {tex}\Delta{/tex}E. The kinetic energy of electron at initial level is not provided and thus it should be assumed to be zero.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} kinetic energy of electron = E = {tex}\Delta E{/tex} = e.V 
Also kinetic energy = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m u^{2}{/tex} = e.V
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}u=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{meV}}{/tex}
From de Broglie equation, {tex}u=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{h}{\lambda}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{meV}}{/tex}

questions.question:
The radius of the second Bohr orbit for hydrogen atom is :
(Planck’s Const, h = 6.6262 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 J-s; mass of electron = 9.1091 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg;
charge of electron e = 1.60210 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C; permittivity of vacuum {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} =8.854185 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12kg-1m-3A2)
questions.explanation:
{tex}r_{n}=r_{0} \frac{n^{2}}{Z}=0.529 \times 4{/tex} (n = 2, Z = 1)
= 2.12 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}

questions.question: If r is the radius of the first orbit, the radius of n th orbit of H-atom is given by:
questions.explanation: {tex}r_{n}=r_{1} \times n^{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The energy of an electron in the nth Bohr orbit of hydrogen atom is:
questions.explanation: {tex}E_{n}=\frac{E_{1}}{n^{2}}{/tex}

questions.question: The radius of hydrogen atom in the ground state is 0.53 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}. The radius of Li2+ ion (atomic number = 3) in a similar state is:
questions.explanation: {tex}r_{\mathrm{Li}^{2+}}=\frac{r_{1 \mathrm{H}}}{3}=\frac{0.53}{3}{/tex} =0.176 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}

questions.question: In a Bohr’s model of an atom, when an electron jumps from n = 1 to =3, how much energy will be emitted or absorbed?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta E=E_{3}-E_{1}=\frac{-13.6}{9}+\frac{-13.6}{1}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{+13.6 \times 8}{9}=12.08 \mathrm{eV}{/tex}
{tex}=12.08 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{erg}{/tex} {tex}=1.936 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{erg}{/tex}

questions.question: The Bohr orbit radius for the hydrogen atom (n = 1) is approximately 0.530 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}. The radius for the first excited state (n = 2) orbit is (in {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex})
questions.explanation: {tex}r_{2}=r_{1} \times 2^{2}=0.530 \times 4{/tex} = 2.120 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}

questions.question:
For given energy, E = 3.03 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19  Joules corresponding wavelength is :
(h = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34Jsec, c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108m/sec)
questions.explanation:
{tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \lambda=\frac{h c}{E}=\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{3.03 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex}
= 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7m = 656 nm

questions.question: In hydrogen atom, energy of first excited state is -3.4 eV. Then find out KE of electron in same orbit of hydrogen atom:
questions.explanation:
E1= -3.4 eV Also K.E. in I orbit = -E1
= +3.4 eV

questions.question: The total energy of the electron in the hydrogen atom in the ground state is -13.6 eV. The KE of this electron is:
questions.explanation: {tex}\because \mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2} m u^{2}=\frac{Z e^{2}}{2 r} ; \mathrm{TE}=-\frac{Z e^{2}}{2 r}{/tex}

questions.question: The energy of second Bohr’s orbit in H-atom is -328 kJ mol-1, hence the energy of fourth Bohr’s orbit would be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}E_{2}=\frac{E_{1}}{2^{2}}=\frac{-328}{2^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}E_{4}=\frac{E_{1}}{4^{2}}=\frac{-328}{4}{/tex} = -82 kJ mol-1

questions.question: According to the Bohr theory, which of the following transitions in the hydrogen atom will give rise to the least energetic photon?
questions.explanation: {tex}\Delta{/tex}E = E6 - E5 is minimum.

questions.question: The total number of atomic orbitals in fourth energy level of an atom is :
questions.explanation: Number of atomic orbitals in 4th energy shell = 42 =16

questions.question: The energies E1 and E2 of two radiations are 25 eV and 50 eV respectively. The relation between their wavelengths i.e.,{tex}\lambda_1{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}E_{1}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{\mathrm{I}}}{/tex} and {tex}E_{2}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{E_{1}}{E_{2}}=\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex}; {tex}\frac{25}{50}=\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{1}=2 \lambda_{2}{/tex}

questions.question: Based on equation E = -2.178 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 J {tex}\left(\frac{Z^{2}}{n^{2}}\right){/tex}, certain conclusions are written. Which of them is not correct?

options.option: For n = 1, the electron has a more negative energy than it does for n = 6 which means that the electron is more loosely bound in the smallest allowed orbit.
Equation can be used to calculate the change in energy when the electron changes orbit
The negative sign in equation simply means that the energy of electron bound to the nucleus is lower than it would be if the electrons were at the infinite distance from the nucleus.
Larger the value of n, the larger is the orbit radius

questions.explanation: Rest all are correct. Electron is more tightly held in 1st orbit.

questions.question: Calculate the energy in joule corresponding to light of wavelength 45 nm : (Planck’s constant h = 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34Js; speed of light c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108ms-1)
questions.explanation: {tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{45 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 4.42 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 J

questions.question: The angular momentum of electron in ‘d’ orbital is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Orbital angular momentum = {tex}\sqrt{l(l+1)} \cdot \hbar{/tex}
for d-orbital l = 2
Therefore orbital angular momentum
{tex}=\sqrt{2(2+1)} h=\sqrt{6} h{/tex}

questions.question: The radii of two of the first four Bohr’s orbits of the hydrogen atom are in the ratio 1 : 4. The energy difference between them may be:
questions.explanation:
The two orbits are either I and II or II and IV
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{r_{n_{2}}}{r_{n_{1}}}=\frac{4}{1}{/tex} and {tex}r_{n} \propto n^{2}{/tex}
Thus {tex}E_{2}-E_{1}=\frac{-13.6}{4}+13.6{/tex} = 10.2 eV
and {tex}E_{4}-E_{2}=-\frac{13.6}{16}+\frac{13.6}{4}{/tex} = 2.55 eV

questions.question: Binding energy of hydrogen atom is 13.6 eV. The binding energy of a singly ionised helium atom is:
questions.explanation: {tex}E_{1 \mathrm{He}^{+}}=E_{1 \mathrm{H}} \times Z^{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The first orbital of H is represented by {tex}\psi=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}}\left(\frac{1}{a_{0}}\right)^{3 / 2} e^{-r / a_{0}}{/tex} where a0 = Bohr’s orbit. The probability of finding the electron at a distance r from the nucleus is:
questions.explanation:
The probability of the electron being in an infinitesimal region dV at a distance r is given as:
Probability {tex}=\psi^{2} d V{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} 1st orbital is spherically symmetrical
{tex}\therefore V=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}{/tex} or dV = 4{tex}\pi r^{2}{/tex} dr
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Probability {tex}=\psi^{2} \cdot 4 \pi r^{2} d r{/tex}

questions.question: If V1 and v2 are the frequency of the series limit of Lyman and Balmer series respectively and v3 is the frequency of the first line of Lyman series, then:
questions.explanation:
For series limit
Lyman series : {tex}v_{1}=R_{\mathrm{H}}\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right]=R_{\mathrm{H}}{/tex}
Balmer series : {tex}v_{2}=R_{H}\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right]=\frac{R_{H}}{4}{/tex}
For I line of Lyman series :
{tex}\mathrm{v}_{3}=R_{\mathrm{H}}\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right]=\frac{3}{4} R_{\mathrm{H}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad v_{1}-v_{2}=R_{H}-\frac{R_{H}}{4}=\frac{3 R_{H}}{4}=v_{3}{/tex}

questions.question: An electron in H atom jumps from 4th energy level to 2nd energy level. The change in potential energy level during transition is:
questions.explanation:
PE = -2KE = 2TE and {tex}T E=-\frac{13.6}{n^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} PE1 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} -13.6 = -27.2 eV
PE2  {tex}=2 \times \frac{-13.6}{4}{/tex} = -6.8 eV
PE3 {tex}=2 \times \frac{-13.6}{9}{/tex} = -3.02 eV
PE4 {tex}=2 \times \frac{-13.6}{16}{/tex} = -1.7 eV
{tex}\therefore{/tex} PE4 - PE2 = -1.7 - (-6.8) = 5.1 eV

questions.question: Which of the following series of transition in the spectrum of hydrogen atom falls in visible region?
questions.explanation: In H-spectrum, Balmer series transitions fall in visible region.

questions.question: The difference between the radii of 3rd and 4th orbits of Li2+ is {tex}\Delta{/tex}R1. The difference between the radii of 3rd and 4th orbits of He+ is {tex}\Delta{/tex}R2. Ratio {tex}\Delta \mathrm{R}_{1}: \Delta \mathrm{R}_{2}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
r = {tex}0.529 \frac{n^{2}}{Z} \mathop A \limits^o{/tex}
For Li2+,
{tex}\left(r_{\mathrm{Li}^{2+}}\right)_{n=4}-\left(r_{\mathrm{Li}^{2+}}\right)_{n=3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.529}{3}\left[4^{2}-3^{2}\right]=\Delta R_{1}{/tex} 
For He+,
{tex}\left(r_{\mathrm{He}^{+}}\right)_{n=4}-\left(r_{\mathrm{He}^{+}}\right)_{n=3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.529}{2}\left[4^{2}-3^{2}\right]=\Delta R_{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{\Delta R_{1}}{\Delta R_{2}}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}

questions.question: The region in the electromagnetic spectrum where the Balmer series lines appear is:
questions.explanation: In the hydrogen spectrum, the maximum lines of the Balmer series lie in the visible region.

questions.question: The shortest wavelength of hydrogen atom in Lyman series is {tex}\lambda{/tex}. The longest wavelength in Balmer series of {tex}{He}^{+}{/tex}is
questions.explanation:
Shortest wavelength {tex}\to{/tex} Max. energy ({tex}\infty{/tex} {tex}\to{/tex} 1)
For Lyman series of H atom,
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}}=R_{\mathrm{H}}(1)^{2}\left[\frac{1}{1}-0\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\lambda_{1}}=R_{\mathrm{H}} \Rightarrow R_{\mathrm{H}}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex} 
For Balmer series of He+,
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R_{\mathrm{H}}(2)^{2}\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\lambda}=R_{\mathrm{H}}(4)\left(\frac{9-4}{36}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\lambda}=\frac{5 R_{\mathrm{H}}}{9} \Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\lambda=\frac{9}{5 R_{\mathrm{H}}}=\frac{9 \lambda}{5}{/tex}

questions.question:
For the Balmer series in the spectrum of H atom {tex}\bar{v}=R_{H}\left\{\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right\}{/tex}, the correct statements among (i) to (iv) are:

  1. As wavelength decreases, the lines in the series converge
  2. The integer n1 is equal to 2
  3. The lines of longest wavelength correspond to n2 = 3
  4. The ionization energy of hydrogen can be calculated from wave number of these lines
questions.explanation: In the Balmer series of H-atom the transition takes place from the higher orbital to n = 2. Therefore the longest wave length corresponds to n1 = 2 and n2 = 3. As the wave length decreases, the lines in the series converges. Hence, statement (i), (ii), (iii) are the correct statements among the given options.

questions.question: The radius of the second Bohr orbit, in terms of the Bohr radius, a0, in Li2+ is:
questions.explanation:
r = {tex}\frac{a_{0} n^{2}}{Z}{/tex}
For Li2+, r = {tex}\frac{a_{0}(2)^{2}}{3}=\frac{4 a_{0}}{3}{/tex}

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is false?

  1. Splitting of spectral lines in electrical field is called Stark effect
  2. The frequency of emitted radiation from a black body goes from a lower wavelength to a higher wavelength as the temperature increases
  3. Photon has momentum as well as wavelength
  4. Rydberg constant has a unit of energy
questions.explanation: When the temperature is increased, the black body emits high-energy radiation from higher wavelength to lower wavelength.

questions.question: If the shortest wavelength in the Lyman series of a hydrogen atom is A, then the longest wavelength in the Paschen series of He+ is:
questions.explanation:
For Lyman series (shortest wavelength)
n1 = 1, n2 = {tex}\infty{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R Z^{2}\left(\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right){/tex},
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{A}=1^{2} R\left(\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right) \Rightarrow \frac{1}{A}{/tex} = R
Longest wavelength = 1st line
n1 = 3, n2 = 4
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R Z^{2}\left(\frac{1}{3^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\lambda}=\frac{R 7}{36}{/tex}
R = {tex}\frac{1}{A}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=\frac{\frac{1}{A} \times 7}{36} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\lambda}=\frac{7}{36 A} \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{36 A}{7}{/tex}

questions.question: If m and e are the mass and charge of the revolving electron in the orbit of radius r for hydrogen atom, the total energy of the revolving electron will be:
questions.explanation:
Total energy of a revolving electron is the sum of its kinetic and potential energy.
Total energy = K.E. + P. E.
= {tex}\frac{e^{2}}{2 r}+\left(-\frac{e^{2}}{r}\right) =-\frac{e^{2}}{2 r}{/tex}

questions.question: The wave number of the first emission line in the Balmer series of H-Spectrum is: (R = Rydberg constant)
questions.explanation:
{tex}\bar{v}=R Z^{2}\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right){/tex}
= {tex}R\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}\right)=\frac{5 R}{36}{/tex}

questions.question: The de-Broglie's wavelength of electron present in first Bohr orbit of H atom is:
questions.explanation:
First Bohr orbit of H atom has radius r = 0.529 {tex}\mathop A \limits^o{/tex}
Also, the angular momentum is quantised.
{tex}\mathrm{mvr}=\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
{tex}2 \pi \mathrm{r}=\frac{h}{\mathrm{mv}}=\lambda{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda=2 \pi \times 0.529 \mathop A\limits^o{/tex}

questions.question: The ionization energy of the hydrogen atom (in the ground state) is x kJ. The energy required for an electron to jump from 2nd orbit to the 3rd orbit will be
questions.explanation:
(IE)H = {tex}E_\infty{/tex}−E1 = -E1 = x volt
Put E1 = {tex}-\frac{K}{n^{2}}=-\frac{K}{1^{2}}{/tex} = -K
{tex}\therefore{/tex} K = x
{tex}\Delta{/tex}E = E3 - E2
= {tex}\frac {-K}{3^2}-(\frac {-K}{2^2}){/tex}
= {tex}K \frac{5}{36}=\frac{5 x}{36}{/tex}

questions.question: The most probable radius (in pm) for finding the electron in He+ is
questions.explanation:
Most probable radius = {tex}\frac {a_0}{Z}{/tex}
For helium ioin, Z = 2
where a0 = 52.9 pm
rmp = {tex}\frac{52.9}{2}{/tex} = 26.45 pm

questions.question: Last line of Lyman series for H-atom has wavelength {tex}\lambda_{1} \stackrel{o}{A}{/tex}, 2nd line of Balmer series has wavelength {tex}\lambda_{2} \stackrel{o}{A}{/tex} then
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex} = R(1)2{tex}\left(\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right){/tex}
and {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex} = R(1)2{tex}\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \lambda_{1}=\frac{1}{R}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_{2}=\frac{16}{3 R}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{16}{\lambda_{2}}=\frac{3}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex}

questions.question: The wavenumber of the first line of the Balmer series of hydrogen is 15200cm−1. The wavenumber of the first Balmer line of Li2+ ion is
questions.explanation:
v for hydrogen like species
= vH {tex}\times{/tex} Z2 = 15200 {tex}\times{/tex} 32 = 15200 {tex}\times{/tex} = 136800 cm-1

questions.question: If the diameter of two different nuclei are in the ratio 1 : 3 then their mass number are in the ratio:
An electron in an atom jumps in such a way that its kinetic energy changes from x to {tex}\frac {x}{4}{/tex}. The change in potential energy will be:
questions.explanation: Change in PE = {tex}-\frac{2 x}{4}{/tex} + (2x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {3}{2}x{/tex}

questions.question: The angular momentum of an electron in a given orbit is J. Its kinetic energy will be:
questions.explanation:
Angular momentum J = mvr
J2 = m2v2r2
or {tex}\frac{J^{2}}{2}=\left(\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}\right){/tex}mr2
or KE = {tex}\frac{J^{2}}{2 m r^{2}}{/tex}

questions.question: If in Bohr's model, for unielectronic atom, time period of revolution is represented as Tn, Z where n represents shell no. and Z represents atomic number then the value of T1,2 : T2,1 will be:
questions.explanation: {tex}T \propto \frac{n^{3}}{Z^{2}}{/tex}; {tex}\frac{T_{1,2}}{T_{2,1}}=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{8}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{32}{/tex}

questions.question: For a hypothetical hydrogen-like atom, the potential energy of the system is given by U(r) = {tex}\frac{-k e^{2}}{r^{4}}{/tex} where r is the distance between the two particles. If Bohr's model of quantization of angular momentum is applicable, then velocity of particle is given by
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{d[U(r)]}{d r}=\frac{4 k e^{2}}{r^{5}}{/tex} = force
{tex}\frac{4 k e^{2}}{r^{5}}=\frac{m v^{2}}{r}{/tex}
and mvr = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
or r = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi m v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{r}=\frac{2 \pi m v}{n h} {/tex}
{tex}4 k e^{2} \times \frac{1}{r^{5}}=\frac{m v^{2}}{r}{/tex}
{tex}2 k e^{2} \times \frac{1}{r^{4}}{/tex} = mv2
{tex}2 k e^{2} \times \frac{16 \pi^{4} m^{4} v^{4}}{n^{4} h^{4}}{/tex} = mv2
v2 = {tex}\frac {n^4h^4}{32ke^2 \pi^4}{/tex}m3
v = {tex}\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \sqrt{2} k e \pi^{2} m^{3 / 2}}{/tex}

questions.question: In a sample of H-atom electrons make transition from 5th excited state to ground state, producing all possible types of photons, then number of lines in infrared, region are
questions.explanation:
Maximum number of lines produced = {tex}\frac{6(6-1)}{2}{/tex}
Out of these 15 lines, 5 lines belong to ultraviolet region and 4 lines are in the visible region and the rest are in the infrared region.

questions.question:
Match the column:

Column I Column II
i. Number of protons in 0.18 g of {tex}{ }_{15}^{30} \mathrm{P}{/tex} a. 7.54 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022
ii. Number of photons having {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 4000 {tex}\overset oA{/tex} and total energy 1.00 J b. 5.24 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022
iii. Quanta of radiation (v= 4.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 1013 s-1) needed to melt 7g of ice if it takes 333 J to melt 1 g of ice c. 2.01 {tex}\times{/tex} 1018
  d. 3.01 {tex}\times{/tex} 1020
questions.explanation:
1. Number of protons in 0.18 g of {tex}{ }_{15}^{30} \mathrm{P}{/tex}:
    0.18 g {tex}\times \frac{1 \mathrm{~mol}^{\prime} \mathrm{P}^{\prime}}{31 \mathrm{~g}^{\prime} \mathrm{P}^{\prime}} \times \frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}{ }^{\mathrm{\prime} \mathrm{P}^{\prime}} \text { atoms }}{1 \mathrm{~mol}^{\prime} \mathrm{P}^{\prime}} \times \frac{15 \text { protons }}{1 \mathrm{atom}^{\prime} \mathrm{P}^{\prime}}{/tex}
    = 5.24 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022 protons
  2. Number of photons having {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 4000 {tex}\overset oA{/tex} and total energy = 1.00 J
    Energy of one photon = {tex}\frac{hc}{\lambda}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{4000 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex}
    Ephoton = 4.96 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
    Number of photons = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\text {total }}}{\mathrm{E}_{\text {photon }}}=\frac{1}{4.16 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex}
    = 2.01 {tex}\times{/tex} 1018
  3. 7 g ice {tex}\times \frac{333 \mathrm{~J}}{1 \mathrm{~g} \text { ice }}{/tex} = 23331 J of energy is needed to melt 7 g of ice
    Energy of one quantum:
    E = hv = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 {tex}\times{/tex} 4.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 1013
    = 3.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-20 J
    Number of quanta needed = {tex}\frac{2331}{3.09 \times 10^{-20}}{/tex}
    = 7.54 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022

questions.question: The threshold frequency of metal is 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 s-1. When the metal is radiated with a frequency ________ the kinetic energy of ejected photoelectrons is 7.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-21J.
questions.explanation:
According to Einstein’s photoelectric equation,
hv = hv0 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}mv2
hv = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 + 7.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-21
v = {tex}\frac{33.13 \times 10^{-48}+7.25 \times 10^{-21}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{/tex}
v = 1.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 1013 s-1

questions.question: The third line in Balmer series corresponds to an electronic transition between ________ Bohr’s orbits in hydrogen.
questions.explanation: 5 {tex}\to{/tex} 2

questions.question: What is the ratio of longest to shortest wavelength in hydrogen spectrum of the Paschen series? (RH = 109677 cm-1)
questions.explanation:
Paschen series n1 = 3 and n2 = 4, 5, 6...
For shortest wavelength, n1 = 3 and n2 = {tex}\infty{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex} = RH {tex}\left[\frac{1}{(3)^{2}}-\frac{1}{(\infty)^{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 8206 {tex}\overset oA{/tex}
For longest wavelength, n1 = 3 and n2 = 4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 18757 {tex}\overset oA{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ratio = {tex}\frac{18757}{8206}{/tex} = 2.3 : 1

questions.question: Which of the following transitions have minimum wavelength?
questions.explanation: For n4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} n1, the greater the transition, greater the energy difference, lesser will be the wavelength.

questions.question: Which of the following transition occurs with the highest energy absorption?
questions.explanation: Absorption is always accompanied by the excitation of electrons from lower energy to higher energy level. Thus, other options can be eliminated. Since, there exists a greater energy difference between 3 and 5 energy levels than between 2 and 3, so this option (n = 3 to n = 5) is the right answer.

questions.question: When a 1.8 g sample of hydrogen atom is irradiated with light, a certain fraction of atoms get excited to n = 3 level and n = 2 level. When the excited atoms fall back to the ground state, the energy evolved is 5.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 J and 2.65 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 J respectively. What % of H atoms do NOT get excited upon irradiation?
questions.explanation:
Number of ‘H’ atoms in 1.8 g H is:
1.8 g 'H' {tex}\times \frac{1 \mathrm{~mol}^{\prime} \mathrm{H}^{\prime}}{1 \mathrm{~g}^{\prime} \mathrm{H}^{\prime}} \times \frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}} \text { atoms }}{1 \mathrm{~mol}^{\prime} \mathrm{H}^{\prime}}{/tex}
= 1.08 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024 atoms
For n = 3 to n = 1 transition,
energy evolved = 5.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 J
i.e., E = (E3 - E1) {tex}\times{/tex} 'X' atoms {tex}\times{/tex} 1.602 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 5.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 105
= {tex}\left[\frac{-13.6}{(3)^{2}}-\frac{-13.6}{(1)^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 'X' atoms {tex}\times{/tex} 1.602 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19
3.54 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024 = [-1.51 + 13.6] {tex}\times{/tex} 'X' atoms
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 'X' = 2.92 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 'H' atoms are excited to n = 3 level
For n = 2 to n = 1 transition,
energy evolved = 2.65 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 J
i.e., E = (E2 - E1) {tex}\times{/tex} 'Y' atoms {tex}\times{/tex} 1.602 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19
= 2.65 {tex}\times{/tex} 105
= {tex}\left[\frac{-13.6}{(2)^{2}}-\frac{-13.6}{(1)^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 'Y' atoms {tex}\times{/tex} 1.602 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19
1.65 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024 = [-3.4 + 13.6] {tex}\times{/tex} 'Y' atoms
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 'Y' = 1.62 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 'H' atoms are excited to n = 2 level
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total excited 'H' atoms = X + Y
= 4.54 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023
% of excited 'H' atoms = {tex}\frac{4.54 \times 10^{2 3}}{1.08 \times 10^{24}} \times{/tex} 100 = 42%
{tex}\therefore{/tex} % of unexcited atoms = 58%

questions.question:
Which of the following is CORRECT regarding the energy gap between different stationary states of hydrogen atom?

  1. (E3 - E2) < (E4 - E2)
  2. (E3 - E2) > (E4 - E2)
  3. (E3 - E2) < (E2 - E1)
  4. (E3 - E2) < (E4 - E3)
questions.explanation: The energy gap between the different stationary states of hydrogen atom decreases as (E2 - E1) > (E3 - E2) > (E4 - E3) and so on.

questions.question: The following phenomena CANNOT be explained by the Bohr model, except:
questions.explanation: Predicting line spectra of hydrogen

questions.question: The energy of a photon is calculated by ________.
questions.explanation: E = hv

questions.question: Radius of Li2+ = ________ the radius of H; and radius of Be3+ = ________ the radius of H
questions.explanation:
For hydrogen-like species, i.e. species containing only one electron, the radius of the nth orbit is given as r = 0.529 {tex}\times \frac{\mathrm{n}^{2}}{\mathrm{Z}} \overset oA{/tex}
r = 0.529 {tex}\times \frac{\mathrm{n}^{2}}{\mathrm{Z}} \overset oA{/tex}
For Li2+ n = 1 and Z = 3
Li = rn = {tex}\frac{1}{3}^{\mathrm{rd}}{/tex} radius of H atom
For Be3+ n = 1 and Z = 4
Be = rn = {tex}\frac{1}{4}^{\mathrm{th}}{/tex} radius of H atom

questions.question: The energy of a 325 nm photon is ________.
questions.explanation:
E = hv = {tex}\frac{hc}{\lambda}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6.626\times10^{-34}\times3\times10^8}{325\times10^{-9}}{/tex}
= 6.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
= {tex}\frac{6.11\times10^{-19}}{1.602\times10^{-19}}{/tex} eV
= 3.8 eV

questions.question: Ionization energy of He+ is ________.
questions.explanation:
For He+; Z = 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I.E. of He+ = (I.E. hydrogen) {tex}\times{/tex} (2)2
= (I.E hydrogen) {tex}\times{/tex} 4

questions.question: The mass of a photon with a wavelength equal to 1.34 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 cm is ________.
questions.explanation:
We know that {tex}\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}{/tex}; {tex}\therefore{/tex} m = {tex}\frac{h}{v\lambda}{/tex}
The velocity of photon (v) = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108m sec-1
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1.34 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8cm = 1.34 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} m = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}}{1.34 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m} \times 3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{msec}^{-1}}{/tex}
= 1.64 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-32 kg (kg = J s2 m-2)

questions.question: Neils Bohr suggested the concept of ________.
questions.explanation: stationary orbits

questions.question: Energy of electron of He+ in second Bohr orbit is ________.
questions.explanation:
For hydrogen-like species, i.e. species containing only one electron, the energy of electron in the nth energy levels can be calculated using following formulae:
En = -(2.18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18) {tex}\times \frac{\mathrm{Z}^{2}}{\mathrm{n}^{2}}{/tex} J atom-1
= -(2.18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18) {tex}\times \frac{2^{2}}{2^{2}}{/tex}
= -2.18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 J/atom

questions.question: An electron in a Bohr’s stationary orbit can go to a higher stationary orbit ________.

options.option: without any absorption or emission of electromagnetic radiation
by absorption of electromagnetic radiation of a particular frequency
by absorption of any electromagnetic radiation

questions.explanation: by absorption of electromagnetic radiation of a particular frequency

questions.question: When an electron falls to a lower orbit, its energy ________.
questions.explanation: decreases

questions.question: If the radius of first Bohr’s orbit of hydrogen atom is a0, then the radius of fourth Bohr’s orbit would be
questions.explanation:
According to Bohr’s theory for a hydrogen atom, a radius of the nth orbit is given by,
rn = 0.529 {tex}\times{/tex} n2 {tex}\overset oA{/tex}
i.e., rn {tex}\propto{/tex} n2
{tex}\therefore \frac{r_{1}}{r_{4}}=\frac{1}{(4)^{2}}{/tex}
The radius of first Bohr’s orbit is given as a0
{tex}\therefore \frac{a_{0}}{r_{4}}=\frac{1}{16}{/tex}
r4 = 16a0

questions.question: What is the wavelength of light (in m) emitted when an electron in a hydrogen atom goes from n = 5 to n = 3?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}E = 2.18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 {tex}\left[\frac{1}{(5)^{2}}-\frac{1}{(3)^{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}E = -1.55 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ephoton = +1.55 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
Now, {tex}\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\mathrm{E}_{\text {photon }}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{1.55 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1.28 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 m

questions.question: The shortest wavelength in hydrogen spectrum of Lyman series is ________. [R = Rydberg constant]
questions.explanation:
For Lyman series, n1 = 1
For shortest wavelength, n2 = {tex}\infty{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{1}{\lambda_{\min }}{/tex} = R{tex}\left(\frac{Z^{2}}{n_{1}^{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{1}{\lambda_{\min }}{/tex} = R{tex}\left(\frac{1^{2}}{1^{2}}\right){/tex} = R
{tex}\therefore\lambda_{min}=\frac{1}{R}{/tex}

questions.question: In hydrogen atom, the de Broglie wavelength of an electron in the second Bohr orbit is ________. [Given that Bohr radius, a0 = 52.9 pm]
questions.explanation:
r = a0n2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} r = 52.9 {tex}\times{/tex} (2)2 pm
Now,
n{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r
{tex}\lambda=\frac{2\pi r}{n}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 \pi \times 52.9 \times(2)^{2}}{2}{/tex}
= 211.6 {tex}\pi{/tex} pm

questions.question:
Find the CORRECT match.

Column I Column II
i. R a. {tex}\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m z e^{2}}{/tex}
ii. {tex}\bar v{/tex} b. {tex}-\left[\frac{2 \pi^{2} m Z^{2} e^{4}}{n^{2} h^{2}}\right]{/tex}
