Which of the following statement is INCORRECT with respect to hydrogen atom?

  1. The energy gap between consecutive energy levels decreases with an increase in principal quantum number.
  2. The radius of the first stationary state is 52.9 pm.
  3. The energy associated with each energy level decreases with an increase in principal quantum number.
  4. The square of orbital wave function at a point gives the probability of finding the electron at that point.
questions.explanation: The energy associated with each energy level decreases with an increase in principal quantum number.

questions.question: Which of the following transitions, for a hydrogen atom, emits a photon of lowest energy?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}E = -R{tex}\left(\frac{1}{\left(n_{f}\right)^{2}}-\frac{1}{\left(n_{i}\right)^{2}}\right){/tex}

  ni nr {tex}\Delta{/tex}E (J) Ephoton (J)
A 4 1 -2.04 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 +2.04 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18

D 2 3 This is an absorption spectrum

questions.question: A ray of white light is spread out into a series of coloured bands of visible light are called
questions.explanation: spectrum

questions.question: In the line spectrum of hydrogen, the lines described by the formula {tex}\bar{v}=109,677\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{n^2}\right) \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex} where, n = interger, n {tex}\geq{/tex} 3 constitutes
questions.explanation: Balmer series

questions.question: The first line in the Balmer series in the H atom will have the frequency
questions.explanation:
Frequency of first line in Balmer series can be calculated as
{tex}\mathrm{v}=3.29 \times 10^{15}\left[\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right] \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex}
Here, n1 = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n2 = 3
= {tex}3.29 \times 10^{15} \times \frac{5}{36}{/tex}
= 4.57 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 s-1

questions.question: An electron is moving in Bohr’s fourth orbit. Its de-Broglie wavelength is {tex}\lambda{/tex}. Calculate the circumference of the fourth orbit.
questions.explanation:
According to Bohr’s concept,
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}\left(\because \lambda=\frac{h}{m v}\right){/tex} and n = 4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Circumference = {tex}2\pi r{/tex} = {tex}4\cdot h/m\nu{/tex} = {tex}4\lambda{/tex}

questions.question:
Major development(s) responsible for the formulation of Bohr's model of atom were

  1. Duel character of the electromagnetic radiation
  2. experimental results regarding atomic spectra which can be explained only by assuming quantised electronic energy levels in atoms
  3. Both (a) and (b)
  4. none of these
questions.explanation: Dual character of the electromagnetic radiation and experimental results regarding atomic spectra which can be explained only by assuming quantised electronic energy levels in atoms.

questions.question: The only series of lines appear in the visible region of the electromagnetic spectrum of hydrogen is
questions.explanation: Balmar series

questions.question: The phenomenon of splitting of spectral lines under the influence of the electric field is called
questions.explanation: Stark effect

questions.question: The energy of an electron in the first Bohr orbit of hydrogen atom is -2.18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 J. Its energy in the third Bohr orbit is ________.
questions.explanation:
En = {tex}\frac{-2.18 \times 10^{-18} Z^2}{n^2}{/tex}
i.e. {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{n}} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^2} \Rightarrow{/tex} Energy in the 3rd Bohr orbit is {tex}\frac 19{/tex}th of energy in first Bohr orbit.

questions.question: The radius of the 2nd orbit of Li2+ is x. The expected radius of the 3rd orbit of Be3+ is
questions.explanation:
rn = {tex}\frac{52.9 \mathrm{n}^2}{\mathrm{Z}}{/tex}pm
Li2+: r2 = x = k {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{2^2}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 \mathrm{k}}{2}{/tex}
Be3+: r3 = y = k {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{3^2}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac 94 \times \frac{3}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{27}{16}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{27}{16}x{/tex}

questions.question:
Identify the incorrect statement from the following.

  1. A circular path around the nucleus in which an electron moves is proposed as Bohr’s orbit.
  2. An orbital is the one electron wave function {tex}(\psi){/tex} in an atom.
  3. The existence of Bohr’s orbits is supported by hydrogen spectrum.
  4. Atomic orbital is characterised by the quantum numbers n and l only.
questions.explanation: Atomic orbital is characterised by n, l, m.

questions.question: Hydrogen has three isotopes: protium (1H), deuterium (2H or D) and tritium (3H or T). They have nearly same chemical properties but different physical properties. They differ in
questions.explanation: Isotopes have same atomic number but different mass number. They have different number of neutrons which results in different mass number.

questions.question: If the radius of the 3rd Bohr’s orbit of hydrogen atom is r3 and the radius of 4th Bohr’s orbit is r4. Then:
questions.explanation:
r = 0.529 {tex}\times \frac{n^2}{Z}{/tex}{tex}\overset oA{/tex}
r3 = 0.529 {tex}\times \frac{3^2}{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r4 = 0.529 {tex}\times \frac{4^2}{1}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{r_4}{r_3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4^2}{3^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{16}{9}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r4 = {tex}\frac{16 r_3}{9}{/tex}

questions.question: Which of the following forms of hydrogen emits low energy {tex}\beta^-{/tex} particles?
questions.explanation: Among the isotopes of hydrogen, only tritium is radioactive and emits low energy {tex}\beta^-{/tex} particles.

questions.question: The electron in the hydrogen atom undergoes transition from higher orbitals to orbital of radius 211.6 pm. This transition is associated with:
questions.explanation:
r = 0.529 {tex}\times \frac{n^2}{Z}{/tex}{tex}\overset oA{/tex}
r = 211.6pm = 2.11{tex}\overset oA{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.529 {tex}\times \frac{n^2}{1}{/tex} = 2.11{tex}\overset oA{/tex} [Z = 1]
n = 2 (Balmer series)

questions.question: The energy of an electron in first Bohr orbit of H-atom is -13.6 eV. The energy value of electron in the excited state of Li2+ is:
questions.explanation:
For Li2+ ion, E = -13.6 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{Z^2}{n^2}{/tex}eV = -13.6 {tex}\times \frac{(3)^2}{(2)^2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{-13.6 \times 9}{4}{/tex} = -30.6 eV

questions.question:
Based on the equation: {tex}\triangle{/tex}E = -2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18J {tex}\left(\frac{1}{n_2^2}-\frac{1}{n_1^2}\right){/tex} the wavelength of the light that must be absorbed to excite hydrogen electron from level n = 1 to level n = 2 will be:
(h = 6.625 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 Js, c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108ms-1)
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}E = -2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 {tex}\times\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{1^2}\right){/tex}
= -2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{-3}{4}{/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18J
{tex}\Delta{/tex}E = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h c}{\Delta E}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.625 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}-\mathrm{s} \times 3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}}{1.5 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{~J}}{/tex} = 1.325 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7m

questions.question: The radius of which of the following orbit is same as that of the first Bohr’s orbit of hydrogen atom?
questions.explanation:
rn = 0.529{tex}\frac{n^2}{Z} \overset oA{/tex}
For hydrogen, n = 1 and Z = 1; {tex}\therefore{/tex} rH = 0.529
For Be3+, n = 2 and Z = 4;
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}r_{\mathrm{Be}^{3+}}{/tex}= {tex}\frac{0.529 \times 2^2}{4}{/tex} = 0.529

questions.question:
The potential energy (Y) curve for H2 formation as a function of internuclear distance (X) of the H atoms is shown below.

The bond energy of H2 is
questions.explanation: Bond energy of H-H bond is the difference in energy due to bond formation. In the given graph, a is the amount of energy inatomic form while b is the amount of energy inbonded form. So, bond energy will be b - a.

questions.question: The energy absorbed by each molecule (A2) of a substance is 4.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J and bond energy per molecule is 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J. The kinetic energy of the molecule per atom will be:
questions.explanation:
Energy absorbed by each molecule = Bond energy per molecule + Kinetic energy per molecule, 4.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J = 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J + Kinetic energy per molecule
0.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 = Kinetic energy per molecule 
Now,
Kinetic energy per atom = {tex}\frac {Kinetic \ \ energy \ \ per \ \ molecule}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{0.4 \times 10^{-19}}{2}{/tex} = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19
= 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-20 J.

questions.question:
The energy of photon is given as: {tex}\Delta{/tex}E/atom = 3.03 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J atom-1, then the wavelength ({tex}\lambda{/tex}) of the photon is:
[Given, h (Planck's constant) = 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 Js, c (velocity of light) = 3.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 ms-1].
questions.explanation:
Expression for the energy of a photon is as follows:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}E = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\Delta{/tex}E = 3.03 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J atom-1, h = 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 Js, c = 3.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 ms-1
Thus, 3.03 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J atom-1 = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js} \times 3.00 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7ms-1
Now 1 m = 109 nm
Hence, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 {tex}\times{/tex} 109 nm
= 6056 {tex}\times{/tex} 102 nm = 656 nm.

questions.question: In the photoelectric emission, the energy of the emitted electron is:

options.option: proportional to the intensity of incident photon.
same as that of the incident photon

questions.explanation: In the photoelectron emission, when a photon is incident on the metal with certain energy then some part of this energy is used to eject the electron from the metal, which is known as threshold energy and the remaining energy is acquired by the emitted electron as its kinetic energy. Thus, the energy of emitted electron is always smaller than the incident photon.

questions.question: When an electron of charge e and mass m moves with a velocity V about the nuclear charge Ze in circular orbit of radius r, the potential energy of the electrons is given by:
questions.explanation:
Potential energy of the electron is the amount of work done in carrying the electron from infinity to the radius r.
Thus, potential energy = {tex}\int\limits_{\infty}^r-\frac{Z e^2}{r^2} d r=-\frac{Z e^2}{r}{/tex}

questions.question:
Radius of the first excited state of helium ion is given as:
{tex}a_{0} \rightarrow{/tex} radius of first stationary state of hydrogen atom.
questions.explanation:
Formula used:
Bohr radius {tex}=a_{0} \times \frac{n^{2}}{z} n^{\text {th }}{/tex} orbit
Where, {tex}n={/tex} No. of orbit
{tex}z=\text { Atomic number }{/tex}
In the first excited state, {tex}{e}^{-}{/tex} jumps from {tex}1^{\text {st }}{/tex} orbit to {tex}2^{\text {nd }}{/tex} orbit.
{tex}\therefore n=2{/tex} and {tex}{z}=2{/tex}
By putting the value,
{tex}r^{n}=a_{0} \times \frac{(2)^{2}}{2}=2 a_{0}{/tex}

questions.question: According to Bohr's model of hydrogen atom, which of the following statement is incorrect?

options.option: Radius of 8th orbit is four times larger than that of 4th orbit.
Radius of 4th orbit is four times larger than that of 2nd orbit.
Radius of 3rd orbit is nine times larger than that of 1st orbit.
Radius of 6th orbit is three time larger than that of 4th orbit.

questions.explanation:
Formula used {tex}r_{n}=a_{0} \times \frac{n^{2}}{{Z}}{/tex}
where, {tex}a_{0}={/tex} Bohr radius
{tex}n={/tex} orbit number
{tex}{Z}={/tex} atomic number

  1. When {tex}n=3, r_{3}=a_{0} \times \frac{3^{2}}{1}=990{/tex}
    Radius of third orbit is nine times larger than that of first orbit.
  2. When {tex}n=8, r_{8}=a_{0} \times \frac{(8)^{2}}{1}=64 a_{0}{/tex}
    {tex}n=4, r_{4}=a_{0} \times \frac{(4)^{2}}{1}=1690{/tex}
    Radius of eighth orbit is four times larger than that of fourth orbit.
  3. When {tex}n=6 r_{6}=a_{0} \times \frac{(6)^{2}}{1}=36 a_{0}{/tex}
    {tex}n=4, r_{4}=a_{0} \times \frac{(4)^{2}}{1}=16 a_{0}{/tex}
    Radius of sixth orbit is four times larger than that of fourth orbit. It is incorrect statement.
  4. When {tex}n=4, r_{4}=a_{0} \times \frac{(4)^{2}}{1}=16 a_{0}{/tex}
    {tex}n=2, r_{4}=a_{0} \times \frac{(2)^{2}}{1}=4 a_{0}{/tex}
    Radius of fourth orbit is four times larger than that of second orbit.

questions.question: The reduction in the attractive interaction of an electron in the outer shell and the nucleus due to the presence of electrons in the inner shells is known as _________.
questions.explanation: The effective nuclear charge experienced by an electron depends upon the shell and the orbital in which the electron is present. The electrons in the outer shell are shielded from the nucleus by the electrons in the inner shells.

questions.question: Due to the presence of electrons in the inner shells, the electron in the outer shell will not experience the full positive charge of the nucleus (Ze). This is known as:

options.option: shielding of the outer shell electrons from the nucleus by the inner shell electrons
charge stealing by outer shell electrons from the inner shell electrons
shielding of the inner shell electrons from the nucleus by the outer shell electrons
charge stealing by inner shell electrons from the outer shell electrons

questions.question: How many electrons can be accommodated in a sub-shell for which n = 3, l = 1?
questions.explanation: In 3p-sub-shell max. no. of electrons = 6.

questions.question: Identify INCORRECT statement from the following:

options.option: Maximum number of electrons in an orbital is two.
An orbit represents the planar motion of an electron.

questions.explanation: Except s-orbitals, all other orbitals have directional characteristics.

questions.question: Which of the following is INCORRECT about an orbit?

options.option: It does not follow Heisenberg’s uncertainty principle
It is a defined circular path around nucleus

questions.explanation: Orbits are non-directional.

topics.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light

questions.question: Correct expression for all series of lines in the hydrogen spectrum is (n1 = 1, 2 , 3...) and n2 = n1 + 1, n1 + 2, ..., and {tex}\bar \nu {/tex} is the wave number)
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light

questions.question: The formula E = h{tex}\nu {/tex} is used to calculate

options.option: radiation emitted by a black body

questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
Max Planck theorized that energy was transferred in chunks known as quanta, equal to {tex}h u{/tex}. The variable h is a constant equal to 6.63 {tex} imes{/tex} 10-34 J·s and the variable {tex} u {/tex}represents the frequency in 1/s.
This equation allows us to calculate the energy of photons, given their frequency.
If the wavelength is given, the energy can be determined by first using the wave equation {tex} (c = \lambda imes u) {/tex} to find the frequency, then using Planck’s equation to calculate energy.

questions.question: Wave number of yellow radiations having a wavelength of 5800 {tex}\mathop A\limits^ \circ {/tex} is ________.
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
Wave number is defined as the reciprocal of the wavelength.
{tex}\bar \nu = {1\over \lambda}{/tex}  
where, {tex}\lambda {/tex} = wavelength ={tex}5800 Å = 5.8 \times 10^{-7} m{/tex}  
{tex}\bar \nu ={1\over 5.8\times 10^{-7}}{/tex}= {tex}1.72\times 10^{6}m^{-1}{/tex}

questions.question: The wavenumber of the spectral line in the emission spectrum of hydrogen will equal to times the Rydberg's constant if the electron jumps from:
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
{tex}\bar \nu = {1\over λ} = RZ^2({1\over n_1^2 }-{ 1\over n_2^2}) {/tex}
where {tex}\lambda{/tex} is the wavelength of the photon
{tex}\bar \nu {/tex} is wave number
R = Rydberg's constant
Z = atomic number of the atom
n1 and n2 are integers where n2 > n1.
For hydrogen, Z = 1.
So, when Electron jump from n=3 to n=1
{tex}\bar \nu = R({1\over 1^2} - {1\over 3^2}){/tex}{tex}=> \bar \nu = R(1-{1\over 9}) = {8\over 9}R{/tex}

questions.question: Find the energy of the photons which correspond to light of frequency 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 Hz (Hint: h = Planck’s constant = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34Js).
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light

questions.question: Find the energy of the photon which has a wavelength of 0.50 Å. (Hint: h = Planck’s constant = 6.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34J).
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light

questions.question: Energy of an electron in stationary state is given by the formula {tex}{{\text{E}}_{{\text{n}} = }}{{\text{R}}_{\text{H}}}\left\{ {1\frac{1}{{{n^2}}}} \right\}{/tex} where n=1,2,3....... . In this equation, RH is called ________.
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light

questions.question: A gas absorbs a photon of 355 nm and emits at two wavelengths. If one of the emissions is at 680 nm, the other is at:
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light

questions.question: Calculate the energy required for the process {tex}{\text{H}}{{\text{e}}^ + }\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} \to {\text{ H}}{{\text{e}}^{{\text{2}} + }}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ }}{{\text{e}}^{\_.}}{/tex} (Hint: The ionization energy of the lowest state, also called the ground state is 2.18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18J atom-1)?
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
He+ ion is a single electron species which resembles like hydrogen. Therefore, the energies of the stationary states of hydrogen-like ions are given by the expression
{tex}E_n ={ 2.18 \times 10^{-18} }\times { Z^2 \over n^2 }{/tex}
Here n = 1 and Z = 2
{tex}E_n = {2.18 \times 10^{-18}}\times {2^2\over 1^2}{/tex} = 8.72 {tex}\times{/tex}10-18J atom-1

questions.question: Which color of light has the greatest energy per photon?
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation: Waves with a short wavelength have the most energy. Red waves have a relatively long wavelength (in the 700 nm range), and violet waves are much shorter - roughly half that. Because violet waves have the shortest wavelength of the visible light spectrum, they carry the most energy.

questions.question: The energy associated with the first orbit in the hydrogen atom is -2.18 {tex}\times{/tex}10-18 J/atom. Calculate the radius of Bohr’s fifth orbit for hydrogen atom.
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light

questions.question: An electron beam undergoes diffraction. This wave character of electrons is used in the making of:
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light

questions.question: The total energy of an electron in the first excited state of the hydrogen atom is about −3.4 eV. What is the kinetic energy of the electron in this state?
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light

questions.question: Photoelectric effect established that light
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation: The emission of free electrons from a metal surface when light is shone on it is called the photoemission or the photoelectric effect. This effect led to the conclusion that light is made up of packets or quantum of energy. Einstein already associated the light quantum with momentum. This strongly supported the particle nature of light and these particles were named photons. Thus, the wave-particle duality of light came into the picture. Einstein won the Nobel Prize for Physics not for his work on relativity, but for explaining the photoelectric effect.

questions.question: Emission spectrum of a material results from the material's (atom or molecules)

options.option: transition from excited to normal state
transition from normal to excited state

questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation: The emission spectrum of a chemical element or chemical compound is the spectrum of frequencies of electromagnetic radiation emitted due to an atom or molecule making a transition from a high energy state to a lower energy state. The photon energy of the emitted photon is equal to the energy difference between the two states. There are many possible electron transitions for each atom, and each transition has a specific energy difference. This collection of different transitions, leading to different radiated wavelengths, makes up an emission spectrum.

questions.question: Absorption spectrum, seen as dark lines in an otherwise continuous spectrum, for the given material results from the material's (atom or molecules):

options.option: absorbing selected wavelengths and returning to normal ground state
absorbing all wavelengths and returning to normal ground state
absorbing selected wavelengths from an input of continuous spectrum
absorbing all wavelengths from an input of continuous spectrum

questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation: In an absorption spectrum, portions of a continuous spectrum (light containing all wavelengths) are missing because they have been absorbed by the medium through which the light has passed; the missing wavelengths appear as dark lines or gaps. Black lines indicating where no light gets through to the element.

questions.question: When an electric discharge is passed through gaseous hydrogen, the H2 molecules dissociate and the energetically excited hydrogen atoms produced emit electromagnetic radiation of discrete frequencies. The hydrogen spectrum consists of

options.option: a continuous spectrum that reduces to zero at small wavelengths
several lines named after their discoverers
a continuous spectrum that reduces to zero at large wavelengths
several series of lines named after their discoverers

questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation: The emission spectrum of hydrogen consists of several series of sharp emission lines in the ultraviolet (Lyman series). in the visible (Balmer series), and in the infrared (Paschen series, Brackett series, etc,) regions of the spectrum. These series are named after their discoverer.

questions.question: Correct expression for all series of lines in the hydrogen spectrum is (n1 = 1, 2, 3... and n2 = n1 + 1, n1 +2, ... , and is the wavenumber)
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
In an amazing demonstration of mathematical insight, in 1885 Balmer came up with a simple formula for predicting the wavelength of any of the lines in atomic Hydrogen in what we now know as the Balmer series.
Three years later, Rydberg generalized this so that it was possible to determine the wavelengths of any of the lines in the hydrogen emission spectrum. Rydberg suggested that all atomic spectra formed families with this pattern (he was unaware of Balmer's work).
It turns out that there are families of spectra following Rydberg's pattern, notably in the alkali metals, sodium, potassium, etc., but not with the precision the hydrogen atom lines fit the Balmer formula, and low values of n2 predicted wavelengths that deviate considerably.
Rydberg's general equation is as follows:
{tex}\overline \nu = {1\over \lambda}=R_h\left ({1\over n_1^2}-{1\over n_2^2}\right){/tex}
where

  1. RH is the Rydberg constant and is 109,737 cm-1
  2. n1 and n2 are integers (whole numbers) with n2>n1

questions.question: The formula E= h{tex}\nu {/tex}is used to calculate:

options.option: radiation emitted by a black body

questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
Max Planck theorized that energy was transferred in chunks known as quanta, equal to {tex}h u{/tex}. The variable h is a constant equal to 6.63 {tex} imes{/tex} 10-34 J·s and the variable {tex} u {/tex}represents the frequency in 1/s.
This equation allows us to calculate the energy of photons, given their frequency.
If the wavelength is given, the energy can be determined by first using the wave equation{tex} (c = \lambda imes u) {/tex}to find the frequency, then using Planck’s equation to calculate energy.

questions.question: Wavenumber of yellow radiations having a wavelength of 5800 {tex}\overset{\circ }{\mathop{A}}\,{/tex} is:
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
Wavenumber is defined as the reciprocal of a wavelength.
{tex}\bar \nu ={1\over \lambda}{/tex}
where, {tex}\lambda {/tex} = wavelength = 5800 Å = 5.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m
So, {tex}\bar \nu ={1\over 5.8\times 10^{-7}}= {/tex} 1.72 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 m-1.

questions.question: Around 1015 Hz corresponds to the region of the electromagnetic spectrum
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation: A frequency of {tex}10^{15} {~Hz}{/tex} primarily corresponds to the ultraviolet (UV) region of the electromagnetic spectrum. The UV region spans frequencies between {tex}10^{14} {~Hz}{/tex} and {tex}10^{17} {~Hz}{/tex}. While visible light falls within {tex}4 \times 10^{14} {~Hz}{/tex} to {tex}7.5 \times{/tex} {tex}10^{14} {~Hz}{/tex}, a frequency around {tex}10^{15} {~Hz}{/tex} is near the upper limit of visible light and is generally considered to be part of the UV spectrum. Thus, {tex}10^{15} {~Hz}{/tex} most accurately belongs to the ultraviolet region.

questions.question: Radio frequency region of the electromagnetic spectrum is used for broadcasting. It is
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation: Radiofrequency (RF) is any of the electromagnetic wave frequencies that lie in the range extending from around 3 kHz to 300 GHz, which include those frequencies used in radio communication or radar. RF usually refers to electrical rather than mechanical oscillations.

questions.question:
Find the energy of the photon which has a wavelength of 0.50 {tex}\mathop A\limits^ \circ {/tex}?
(Hint: h = Planck’s constant = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 J)
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
Energy (E) of a photon having wavelength ({tex}\lambda{/tex}) is given by the expression, {tex}E={hc\over \lambda}{/tex}
where, h = Planck’s constant = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34
c = velocity of light in vacuum = 3 {tex}\times{/tex}108 m/s 
Wavelength {tex}\lambda = 0.50 A^° = 0.50\times 10^{-10} m{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0.50 Å = 0.50{tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 m
Substituting the values in the given expression of E:
{tex}6.626\times 10^{-34}\times 3\times 10^8\over 0.50\times 10^{-10}{/tex} = 3.98 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15 J

questions.question:
Energy of an electron in stationary state is given by the formula
{tex}{E_{{n^m}}}{R_H}\left( {1 - \frac{1}{{{{\text{n}}^2}}}} \right){/tex}
where n = 1,2,3........ In this equation, RH is called
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation: The Rydberg constant, symbol {tex}R_\infty{/tex} for heavy atoms or RH for hydrogen, named after the Swedish physicist Johannes Rydberg, is a physical constant relating to atomic spectra, in the science of spectroscopy.

questions.question: Lines in the hydrogen spectrum which appear in the ultraviolet region of the electromagnetic spectrum, then they are called as:
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation: The Lyman series is a hydrogen spectral series of transitions and resulting ultraviolet emission lines of the hydrogen atom as an electron goes from n {tex}\ge {/tex} 2 to n = 1 (where n is the principal quantum number), the lowest energy level of the electron.

questions.question: Lines in the hydrogen spectrum which appear in the infrared region of the electromagnetic Spectrum, then they are called as
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation: The Lyman series lies in the ultraviolet, whereas the Paschen, Brackett, and Pfund series lies in the infrared.

questions.question: Lines in the hydrogen spectrum which appear in the visible region of the electromagnetic Spectrum, then they are called as:
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation: The Balmer series or Balmer lines in atomic physics is the designation of one of a set of six named series describing the spectral line emissions of the hydrogen atom. The Balmer series is calculated using the Balmer formula, an empirical equation discovered by Johann Balmer in 1885 Balmar series lies in the visible region of the Electromagnetic spectrum.

questions.question: The electrons are ejected from the metal surface when the light of certain frequencies strikes the surface is called ________.
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation: Electrons are ejected from the metal when the light of a certain frequency strikes the surface of a metal, This phenomenon is known as the photoelectric effect and the ejected electrons are called       photoelectrons.

questions.question: When an electron from an excited hydrogen atom undergoes the transition from energy level  n = 4  to  n = 2, the emission of radiations will give rise to:
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation: The spectral lines which arise due to the transition of electrons from higher energy levels to the second energy level will appear in the visible region. Balmer series lies in the visible region of the EM spectrum.

questions.question: The energy associated with the first orbit in the hydrogen atom is -2.18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 J/atom. What is the energy associated with the fifth orbit?
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
The energy of first (Bohr) orbit in hydrogen atom = -2.17 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18Jatom-1
The energy of the fifth orbit will be given by {tex}E_n= E_1\times {Z^2\over N^2}{/tex}
 {tex}E_5={-2.17\times 10^{-18}\over 5^2}=-8.68\times 10^{-20}Jatom^{-1}{/tex}.
The energy of the fifth orbital = {tex}=-8.68\times 10^{-20}Jatom^{-1}{/tex}

questions.question: The wave character of electrons is used in the construction of
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation: The electron microscope uses a beam of accelerated electrons as a source of illumination. The wavelength of an electron being much shorter than the visible light the electron microscope has a higher resolving power.

questions.question: In the emission spectrum of hydrogen atom, the Balmer series falls in the
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation: The spectral lines obtained as a result of transition of electrons from higher energy levels to the second energy level of a hydrogen atom give rise to Balmer Series which is in the visible region of electromagnetic spectrum.

questions.question:
What is the work function of the metal, if the light of wavelength 4000 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex} generates photoelectron of velocity 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 ms-1 from it?
(Mass of electron = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg
Velocity of light = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 ms-1
Planck’s constant = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 Js
Charge of electron = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 JeV-1)
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
Work function of metal ({tex}\phi{/tex}) = hv0
where, v0 = threshold frquency
Also, {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_{e} v^{2}=h v-h v_{0}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_{e} v^{2}=h v-\phi{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\frac{1}{2} m_{e} v^{2}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}-\phi{/tex} ...(ii)
Given: {tex}\lambda=4000 \mathrm{\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} }=4000 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
 v = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 ms-1,
{tex}m_{e}=9 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{kg}, c=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}{/tex}
{tex}h=6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}{/tex}
Thus, on substituting all the given values in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{kg} \times\left(6 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{J} \mathrm{s} \times 3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}}{4000 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{m}}-\phi{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \phi=1.62 \times 10^{-21} \mathrm{kgm}^{2} \mathrm{s}^{-2}-4.96 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}{/tex}
= 3.36 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J [1 kgm2s-2 = 1J]
= 2.1 eV

questions.question: Which of the graphs shown below does not represent the relationship between the incident light and the electron ejected from the metal surface?
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
Since the number of electrons ejected depends on the intensity of light, if the incident light has a frequency more than the threshold frequency. So, the graph in option is correct.
The kinetic energy of electrons depends on frequency of the incident radiation. Hence the graph in option is correct. 
Kinetic energy depends upon two factors frequency as well as threshold frequency. Hence it is clear that it will be a straight line curve but with some intercept, it cannot be the one which directly passes through origin. Hene, the graph of the option is incorrect.
Kinetic energy depends upon two factors frequency as well as the threshold frequency. Hence, the graph of the option is correct.

questions.question: Which of the following does not characterise X-rays?

options.option: Deflected by electric and magnetic fields
Have wavelengths shorter than ultraviolet rays

questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation: X-rays can ionise gases and cannot get deflected by electric and magnetic fields, wavelength of these rays is 150 to 0.1 {tex}\overset oA{/tex}. Thus, the wavelength of X-rays is shorter than that of U.V. rays.

questions.question: Electromagnetic radiation with maximum wavelength is

questions.question: A photon of 300 nm is absorbed by a gas and then reemits two photons. One reemitted photon has wavelength 496 nm, the wavelength of second reemitted photon is:

questions.question: Energy of an electron is given by {tex}E=-2.178 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{~J}\left(\frac{Z^{2}}{n^{2}}\right){/tex} Wavelength of light required to excite an electron in an hydrogen atom from level n = 1 to n = 2 will be: (h = 6.62 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34Js and c = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 108ms-1)

{tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=2.178 \times 10^{-18} \times Z^{2}\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right](Z=1){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \lambda=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{2.178 \times 10^{-18}} \times \frac{4}{3}=1.214 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}

questions.question: Photoelectric emission is observed from a surface for frequencies {tex} u_1{/tex} and {tex} u_2{/tex} of the incident radiation ({tex} u_1{/tex} > {tex} u_2{/tex}). If the maximum kinetic energies of the photoelectrons in the two cases are in the ratio 1 : k then the threshold frequency v0 is given by:
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
{tex}h v_{1}=h v_{0}-\frac{1}{2} m u_{1}^{2}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}h v_{2}=h v_{0}-\frac{1}{2} m u_{2}^{2}{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2} m u_{1}^{2}=\frac{1}{k}\left(\frac{1}{2} m u_{2}^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} from (i) {tex}h v_{1}=h v_{0}-\frac{1}{2 k} m u_{2}^{2}{/tex} ...(iii)
or {tex}\frac{1}{2} m u_{2}^{2}=k h v_{0}-k h v_{1}{/tex} ...(iv)
By (ii) and (iv)
{tex}h v_{2}=h v_{0}-k h v_{0}+k h v_{1}{/tex}
or {tex}v_{0}(1-k)=v_{2}-k v_{1}{/tex}
or {tex}v_{0}=\frac{k v_{1}-v_{2}}{(k-1)}{/tex}

questions.question: An electronic transition from excited state to ground state in one or more steps emits 10 lines. The number of lines falling in UV spectrum are:
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light

{tex}\Sigma \Delta n{/tex} = 10
or {tex}\Sigma{/tex}(n - 1) = 10
or {tex}\Sigma 4{/tex} = 10
{tex}\therefore{/tex} n = 5
Now, four lines from 5 to 1, 4 to 1, 3 to 1 and 2 to 1 will lead for Lyman series falling is regions.

questions.question: A proton and an {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle are accelerated through a potential difference of 100V. The ratio of the wavelength proton and an {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle is:
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
de Broglie wave length, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{\lambda_{p}}{\lambda_{\alpha}}=\frac{p_{\alpha}}{p_{p}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{\alpha} \cdot E_{\alpha}}}{\sqrt{2 m_{p} \cdot E_{p}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{m_{\alpha} \cdot q_{\alpha} \cdot V}}{\sqrt{m_{p} \cdot q_{p} \cdot V}}=\frac{\sqrt{4 \times 2 \times V}}{\sqrt{1 \times 1}}=2 \sqrt{2}{/tex}

questions.question: Ejection of the photoelectron from metal in the photoelectric effect experiment can be stopped by applying 0.5 V when the radiation of 250 nm is used. The work function of the metal is:
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light

{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 250 nm
E = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}=\frac{1240 \mathrm{eV} \cdot \mathrm{nm}}{250 \mathrm{~nm}}{/tex} = 4.96 eV
KE = stopping potential = 0.5 eV
E = W0 + K.E.
4.96 = W0 + 0.5
W0 = 4.46 {tex}\approx{/tex} 4.5eV

questions.question: If {tex}\lambda_0{/tex} and {tex}\lambda{/tex} be threshold wavelength and wavelength of incident light, the velocity of photoelectron ejected from the metal surface is:
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
The kinetic energy of the ejected electron is given by the equation
{tex}h v=h v_{0}+\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} 
or {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{n}}+\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}-\frac{h c}{\lambda_{n}}{/tex} = {tex}h c\left(\frac{\lambda_{0}-\lambda}{\lambda \lambda_{0}}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}v^{2}=\frac{2 h c}{m}\left(\frac{\lambda_{0}-\lambda}{\lambda \lambda_{0}}\right){/tex}
or {tex}v=\sqrt{\frac{2 h c}{m}\left(\frac{\lambda_{0}-\lambda}{\lambda \lambda_{0}}\right)}{/tex}

questions.question: Let v1 be the frequency of the series limit of the Lyman series, v2 be the frequency of the first line of the Lyman series, and v3 be the frequency of the series limit of the Balmer series, then
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
v1 = RcZ2{tex}\left(\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right){/tex}
v1 = RcZ2{tex}\left(\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right){/tex} = RcZ2
v2 = RcZ2{tex}\left(\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right)=\frac{3 R c Z^{2}}{4}{/tex}
v3 = RcZ2{tex}\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right)=\frac{2 R c Z^{2}}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} v1 - v2 = v3

questions.question:
Photon having wavelength 310 nm is used to break the bond of A2 molecule having bond energy 288kgmol-1 then % of energy of photon converted to the K.E. is
[hc = 12400 ev{tex}\stackrel{o}{A}{/tex}, 1ev = 96 kJ/mol]

Energy of on photon
= {tex}\frac{12400}{3100}{/tex} = 4 eV = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 96 = 384 kJ mol-1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} % of energy converted to
K.E. = {tex}\frac{384-288}{384}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{96}{384} \times 100{/tex} = 25

questions.question: Photoelectric emission is observed from a surface for frequencies {tex} u_1{/tex} and {tex} u_2{/tex} of the incident radiation({tex} u_1{/tex} − {tex} u_2{/tex}). If the maximum kinetic energies of the photoelectrons in the two cases are in the ratio 1 : k then the threshold frequency {tex} u_1{/tex} is given by
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
When frequency is {tex}\nu_1{/tex},
h{tex}\nu_1{/tex} = {tex}h v_{0}+\frac{1}{2} m u_{1}^{2}{/tex} ...(i)
When frequency is v2,
h{tex}\nu_2{/tex} = {tex}h v_{0}+\frac{1}{2} m u_{2}^{2}{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}\because \frac{1}{2} m u_{1}^{2}-\frac{1}{k}\left(\frac{1}{2} m u_{2}^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} from Eq. (i)
h{tex}\nu_1{/tex} = {tex}h v_{0}+\frac{1}{2 k} m u_{2}^{2}{/tex} ...(iii)
or {tex}\frac{1}{2} m u_{2}^{2}{/tex} = kh{tex}\nu_1{/tex} - khv0 ...(iv)
From Eqs. (ii) and (iv)
h{tex}\nu_2{/tex} = hv0 + kh{tex}\nu_1{/tex} - khv0
or v0(1 − k) = {tex}\nu_2{/tex} − k{tex}\nu_1{/tex}
or vo = {tex}\frac{k v_{1}-v_{2}}{k-1}{/tex}

questions.question: A hydrogen atom in the ground state is excited by monochromatic radiation of wavelength {tex}\lambda \stackrel{o}{A}{/tex}. The resulting spectrum consists of maximum 15 different lines. What is the wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex}?
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
Total number of spectral lines given by
{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}[n - 1] {tex}\times{/tex} n = 15
{tex}\therefore{/tex} n = 16
Thus, the electron is excited upto 6th energy level from the ground state. Therefore,
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R_{H}\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}109737 \times \frac{35}{36}{/tex};
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 9.373 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 cm = 937.3 {tex}\stackrel{o}{A}{/tex}

questions.question: How many waves are present in 1 km length of a radio wave having frequency 155 {tex}\times{/tex} 1017 Hz?
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
Frequency v = 155 {tex}\times{/tex} 1017 Hz (i.e., s-1)
Wave number is the number of waves present in 1 cm length of a wave
Wavenumber {tex}\bar{v}=\frac{1}{\lambda}=\frac{v}{c}=\frac{155 \times 10^{17}}{3 \times 10^{8} \times 10^{2} \mathrm{~cm}}{/tex}
= 51.66 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 cm-1
Wave number {tex}\bar v{/tex} = 51.66 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 cm-1
No. of waves present in 1 km is
= 51.66 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 = 5.16 {tex}\times{/tex} 1013

questions.question: How many photons are emitted by green light of wavelength 525 nm, if total energy of the light is 189 kJ?
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
Etotal = 189 kJ
Ephoton = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{525 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex}
Ephoton = 3.79 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
Number of photons = {tex}\frac{E_{\text {total }}}{E_{\text {photon }}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{189 \times 10^{3}}{3.79 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex}
= 4.98 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 photons

questions.question: What is the frequency of emitted light, if 598.53 kJ energy is released by 3 moles of photons?
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
Energy of one-mole photon
= {tex}\frac{598.53 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~J}}{3 \mathrm{~mol} \text { photons }} \times \frac{1 \mathrm{~mol} \text { photon }}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \mathrm{photons}}{/tex}
= 3.31 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
Now, E = hv
{tex}\therefore{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{3.31 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}}{/tex}
v = 4.99 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 s-1

questions.question: If a 200-watt bulb emits monochromatic light of wavelength 500 nm, calculate the number of photons emitted per second by the bulb.
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
Energy of photon = hv = {tex}\frac{hc}{\lambda}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js} \times 3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}}{500 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js} \times 3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}}{5 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{19.878 \times 10^{-26}}{5 \times 10^{-7}}{/tex}J = 3.9756 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19J
Power of the bulb = 200 watt = 200 Js-1
{tex}\because{/tex} Energy of 1 photon = 3.9756 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19J
{tex}\therefore{/tex} No. of photons emitted = {tex}\frac{200 \mathrm{Js}^{-1}}{3.9756 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}}{/tex}
= 50.31 {tex}\times{/tex} 1019 s-1
= 5.031 {tex}\times{/tex} 1020 s-1

questions.question: Threshold frequency of a certain metal M is 6.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 Hz. If the wavelength of yellow, green, blue and violet radiation is 576, 535, 454 and 410 nm respectively, then shining which of the following light will result in the ejection of photoelectrons?

questions.question: The ratio of the frequency of radiation with wavelength 400 nm to the frequency of radiation with wavelength 760 nm is ________.
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light

For any two electromagnetic radiations, {tex} u_1{/tex}{tex}\lambda_1{/tex} = {tex} u_1{/tex}{tex}\lambda_2{/tex} 
{tex} u_1{/tex}{tex}\lambda_1{/tex} = {tex} u_1{/tex}{tex}\lambda_2{/tex} [{tex} ecause{/tex} c is constant]

{tex} herefore{/tex} Ratio, {tex} rac{ u_1}{ u_2}= rac{760}{400}{/tex}
= 1.9 : 1 {tex}\cong{/tex}2 : 1

questions.question:
Arrange the following radiations in increasing order of their energies. Wavelength of the radiations are given below.

  1. 300 nm
  2. 300 {tex}\mu{/tex}m
  3. 3 nm
  4. 30 {tex}\overset oA{/tex}
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
1. 300 nm = 300 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9m = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7m
  2. 300 {tex}\mu{/tex}m = 300 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6m = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4m

  4. 30 {tex}\overset oA{/tex} = 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10m = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9m

Increasing order of wavelength is, iii = iv < i < ii
Now, E = {tex}\frac{hc}{\lambda}{/tex} i.e., E {tex}\propto\frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Increasing order of energy is, ii < i < iii = iv

questions.question: If an electron has a wavelength 6.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 m, then kinetic energy associated with the electron will be ________. (mass of electron = 9.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg)
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
According to de Broglie,
{tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{mv}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{m} \lambda}=\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{kgm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg} \times 6.25 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} v = 1.16 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 ms-1
Now,
K.E. = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}mv2
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 9.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg {tex}\times{/tex} (1.16 {tex}\times{/tex} 105)2 m2 {tex}\frac{1}{s^2}{/tex}
= 6.13 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-21 kg {tex}\frac{m^2}{s^2}{/tex}
= 6.13 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-21 J

questions.question: A gas absorbs a photon of wavelength 355 nm and emits at two different wavelengths. If one emission is at 680 nm, the other is at:
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
When an atom absorbs radiation of frequency, v or wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} and emits two photons of frequency v1 and v2 or wavelength {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_2{/tex}, then v = v1 + v2 or {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}}+\frac{1}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore\frac{1}{\lambda}=\frac{1}{\lambda_1}+\frac{1}{\lambda_2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{355}=\frac{1}{680}+\frac{1}{\lambda_2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{1}{355}-\frac{1}{680}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{680-355}{355\times680}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore\lambda_2{/tex} = 743 nm

questions.question: The wavelength of a microscopic particle of mass 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg is 182 nm, its kinetic energy in J is ________.
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
{tex}\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{KE} \cdot \mathrm{m}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \lambda^{2}=\frac{\mathrm{h}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{KE} \cdot \mathrm{m}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} K.E. = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{h}^{2}}{2 \times \mathrm{m} \times \lambda^{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\left(6.625 \times 10^{-34}\right)^{2}}{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times\left(1.82 \times 10^{-7}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{43.89 \times 10^{-68}}{60.287 \times 10^{-45}}{/tex}
= 7.28 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24 J

questions.question: If an electron is travelling at 0.5 % of the speed of light, what will be the linear momentum of the electron?
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
Velocity of the electron is
v = {tex}\frac{0.5}{100}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m s-1 = 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 m s-1
Linear momentum of the electron = mv
= 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 105
= 1.36 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24 kg m s-1

questions.question: The number of photons emitted in 10 hrs by a 60 W sodium lamp is ________. ({tex}\lambda{/tex} photon = 5893 {tex}\overset oA{/tex})
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 5893 {tex}\overset oA{/tex} = 5893 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 m
Energy of the photon = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{5893 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex}J
= 3.37 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19J
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total energy emitted by Na-lamp
= watt {tex}\times{/tex} time
= 60 {tex}\times{/tex} 36000 s
= 2.16 {tex}\times{/tex} 106J
No. of photons = {tex}\frac{E_{\text {lotal }}}{E_{\text {photon }}}=\frac{2.16 \times 10^{6}}{3.37 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex}
= 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024

questions.question: 7.52 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J of energy is atleast required to overcome the attractive forces between an electron and the surface of Ag-metal. The maximum kinetic energy of electron ejected from Ag on exposure to UV light of {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 360 {tex}\overset oA{/tex} is ________.
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
Energy absorbed = {tex}\frac{hc}{\lambda}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-27} \times 3 \times 10^{16}}{360 \times 10^{-8}}{/tex}
= 5.52 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 erg
= 5.52 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 J
Eabsorbed = Eovercome attractive forces + K.Eelectron
{tex}\therefore{/tex} K.E. = 5.52 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 - 7.52 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19
= 47.68 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J

questions.question:
Given is a table of work function {tex}(\phi){/tex} of some metals. How many metals will show photoelectric effect when 300 nm light is incident on the metal?

Metal {tex}\phi{/tex} (ev)
Li 2.4
Na 2.3
K 2.2
Mg 3.7
Cu 4.8
Ag 4.3
Fe 4.7
Pt 6.3
W 4.75
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
The energy of photon = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{h c}{e \lambda}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{300 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex}
= 4.14 eV
For the photoelectric effect to occur, the energy of incident photons must be greater than the work function of the metal.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Only Li, Na, K and Mg have {tex}\phi{/tex} < 4.14 eV

questions.question: Red FM, Radio Mirchi and Vividh Bharti radio stations broadcast at frequencies 93.5 MHz, 98.3 MHz and 100.1 MHz respectively. What is the ratio of wavenumbers of red FM, Radio Mirchi and Vividh Bharti?
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
Wave number {tex}\bar v=\frac{v}{c}{/tex}
1 MHz = 106Hz (i.e., s-1)
For red FM, {tex}\bar{v}=\frac{93.5 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}}{3 \times 10^{1} \times 10^{2} \mathrm{~cm} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}}{/tex}
= 31.166 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 cm-1
For radio mirchi, {tex}\bar{v}=\frac{98.3 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~s}^{-4}}{3 \times 10^{8} \times 10^{2} \mathrm{cms}^{-1}}{/tex}
= 32.766 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 cm-1
For vividh bharti, {tex}\bar{v}=\frac{100.1 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}}{3 \times 10^{8} \times 10^{2} \mathrm{~cm} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}}{/tex}
= 33.366 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 cm-1
Ratio of wave numbers
= Red FM: Radio mirchi : Vividh bharti
= 31.66 : 32.766 : 33.366
Simple ratio
{tex}=\frac{31.166 \times 10^{-4}}{31.166 \times 10^{-4}}: \frac{32.766 \times 10^{-4}}{31.166 \times 10^{-4}}: \frac{33.366 \times 10^{-4}}{31.166 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Simpel ratio = 1 : 1.05 : 1.07

questions.question: Yellow and red colour street light have wavelengths 580 nm and 680 nm respectively. What is the total energy per mole of these photons?
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
Ephoton = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Emole photon = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}} \cdot \mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}{/tex}
Eyellow = {tex}\frac{\left(6.022 \times 10^{23} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right)\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}\right)}{580 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}}{/tex}
= 2.06 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 J mol-1
Ered = {tex}\frac{\left(6.022 \times 10^{23} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right)\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}\right)}{680 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}}{/tex}
= 1.76 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 J mol-1
Total energy = (2.06 {tex}\times{/tex} 105) + (1.76 {tex}\times{/tex} 105)
Total energy = 3.82 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 J

questions.question: If ionisation potential for hydrogen atom is 13.6 eV, then ionisation potential for He+ will be:
questions.topicName: Dual Nature of Matter and Light
questions.explanation:
Expression for ionization energy for hydrogen or hydrogen like atoms is as follows:
E = {tex}\frac{2 \pi^2 m e^4 Z^2}{n^2 h^2}{/tex}
For hydrogen, Z = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} EH = {tex}\frac{2 \pi^2 m e^4(1)^2}{n^2 h^2}{/tex} = 13.6 eV ...(i)
For He+ ion, Z = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} EHe+ = {tex}\frac{2 \pi^2 m e^4(2)^2}{n^2 h^2}{/tex} = 13.6 (2)2 {{tex}\therefore{/tex} From (i)}
= 54.4 eV.

questions.question: de-Broglie equation is
questions.explanation:
Louis de-Broglie proposed that matter,  like light , has a dual character.It exhibits wave as well as  particle nature. The wavelength of the wave associated with a particle of mass m moving with velocity v is given by
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{m v}{/tex}

questions.question:
If p is the momentum of the fastest electron ejected from a metal surface after the irradiation of light having wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex}, then for 1.5 p momentum of the photoelectron, the wavelength of the light should be
(Assume kinetic energy of ejected photoelectron to be very high in comparison to work function)
questions.explanation:
The expression of kinetic energy of photo electrons,
{tex}\mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=E-E_{0}{/tex}
When, KE >> E0, the equation becomes,
{tex}\mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=E{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{h c}{\lambda} \Rightarrow \frac{p^{2}}{2 m^{2}}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=h c \times 2 m^{2} \times \frac{1}{p^{2}} \Rightarrow \lambda \propto \frac{1}{p^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex} = energy of incident light.
E0 = threshold energy or work functions,
{tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{(m v)^{2}}{m^{2}}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{p^{2}}{m^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} p = momentum =mv
As per the given condition,
{tex}\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}=\left(\frac{p_{1}}{p_{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda}=\left(\frac{p}{1.5 \times p}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2}=\frac{4}{9}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda_{2}=\frac{4}{9} \lambda \quad\left[\begin{array}{c} {\because \lambda_{1}=\lambda} \\ {p_{1}=p} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

questions.question: A stream of electrons from a heated filament was passed between two charged plates kept at a potential difference V esu. If e and m are charge and mass of an electron, respectively, then the value of {tex}\frac{h }{\lambda}{/tex} (where, {tex}\lambda{/tex} is wavelength associated with electron wave) is given by
questions.explanation:
de-Broglie wavelength for an electron {tex}(\lambda)=\frac{h}{p}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = {tex}\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} ...(i)
Kinetic energy of an electron = eV
As we know that, KE = {tex}\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} eV = {tex}\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}{/tex} or p = {tex}\sqrt{2m e V}{/tex} ...(ii)
From equation (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\frac{h}{\lambda}={/tex}{tex}\sqrt{2m e V}{/tex}

questions.question: If the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron in nth Bohr orbit in a hydrogenic atom is equal to 1.5 {tex}\pi a_{0}{/tex} (a0 is Bohr radius), then the value of {tex}\frac{n}{Z}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Number of waves = {tex}\frac{\text { Circumference }}{\text { Wavelength }} \Rightarrow n=\frac{2 \pi r}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\begin{aligned} \therefore\ 2 \pi r=n \lambda \end{aligned}{/tex} ...(i)
Also, we know that radius (r) of an atom is given by {tex}r=\frac{a_{0} n^{2}}{Z}{/tex}
Thus, Eq. (i) becomes
{tex}2 \pi a_{0} \frac{n^{2}}{Z}=n \lambda{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}2 \pi a_{0} \frac{n^{2}}{Z}=n\left(1.5 \pi a_{0}\right)\left[\text { Given }, \lambda=1.5 \pi a_{0}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{n}{Z}=\frac{1.5 \pi a_{0}}{2 \pi a_{0}}=\frac{1.5}{2}{/tex} = 0.75

questions.question: The de-Broglie wavelength ({tex}\lambda{/tex}) associated with a photoelectron varies with the frequency ({tex}\nu{/tex})of the incident radiation as, [{tex}\nu_o{/tex}is threshold frequency]
questions.explanation:
de-Broglie wavelength ({tex}\lambda{/tex}) for electron is given by
{tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m \mathrm{K} \cdot \mathrm{E}}}{/tex} ...(i)
Also, according to photoelectric effect
KE = hv -hv0
On substituting the value of KE in Eq (i), we get
{tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m \times\left(h v-h v_{0}\right)}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ \lambda \propto \frac{1}{\left(\nu-\nu_{0}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex}

questions.question: The wavelength associated with a golf ball weighing 200 g and moving at a speed of 5 m/h is of the order

Given, h = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 Js, m = 200 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 kg
v = {tex}\frac{5}{60 \times 60}{/tex}m/s
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{200 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 /(60 \times 60)}{/tex} = 2.38 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 m

λ=100v

questions.question: Planck’s constant has the same dimension as that of
questions.explanation: The Planck constant has dimensions of physical action; i.e., energy multiplied by time, or momentum multiplied by distance or angular momentum. In SI units, the Planck constant is expressed in joule-seconds {tex} (J.s \ or \ N⋅m⋅s \ or \ kg⋅m^2⋅s^{−1}){/tex} .

questions.question: Planck’s constant can be directly measured by
questions.explanation:
The energy of an individual photon is given by E=h⋅ν; where h is Plank's constant and ν is the frequency of the photon.
If the photon's energy exceeds the binding energy of a surface electron (typically in a metal), then the electron can be ejected from the surface. Any energy in excess of the binding energy appears as the kinetic energy of the ejected electron. This is the photoelectric effect.
