questions.question: The de Broglie wavelength of a particle with mass 1 g and velocity 100 m/s is:
questions.explanation: {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{m u}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{1 \times 10^{-3} \times 100}=6.63 \times 10^{-33} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}

questions.question: The value of Planck’s constant is 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 J-s. The velocity of light is 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 ms-1. Which value is closest to the wavelength in nanometre of a quantum of light with frequency of 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 s-1?
questions.explanation: {tex}v=\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex}, {tex}\therefore \lambda=\frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{8 \times 10^{15}}=4 \times 10^{-8}{/tex}

questions.question: The value of Planck’s constant is 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 J-s. The velocity of light is 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m s-1. Which value is closest to the wavelength in nanometre of a quantum of light with frequency of 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 s-1?
questions.explanation: {tex}v=\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex}, {tex}\therefore \lambda=\frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{8 \times 10^{15}}=4 \times 10^{-8}{/tex}

questions.question: A 0.66 kg ball is moving with a speed of 100 m/s. The associated wavelength will be :
questions.explanation: {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{m u}=\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{0.66 \times 100}=1 \times 10^{-35} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}

questions.question: The value of Planck’s constant is 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 J-s. The speed of light is 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1017 nm s-1. Which value is closest to the wavelength in nanometer of a quantum of light with frequency of 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 s-1?
questions.explanation:
{tex}E=h v=\frac{h \cdot c}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} or {tex}\lambda=\frac{c}{v}=\frac{3 \times 10^{17} \times 10^{-9}}{6 \times 10^{15}}=5 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
= 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 nm = 50nm

questions.question: When photons of energy 4.25 eV strike the surface of a metal A, the ejected photoelectrons have maximum kinetic energy, TA (expressed in eV), and de Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda_1{/tex}. The maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons liberated from another metal B by photons of energy 4.20 V is TB - TA - 1.50 eV. If the de Broglie wavelength of these photoelectrons is {tex}\lambda_B{/tex} = 2{tex}\lambda_A{/tex}. The work function of A is 2.25 even which is not correct?

options.option: The work function of B is 3.70 eV
The work function of A is 2.25 eV

questions.explanation:
{tex}u_{A}=\frac{h}{\lambda_{A} \cdot m}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{u_{A}}{u_{B}}=\frac{\lambda_{B}}{\lambda_{A}}=2{/tex} {tex}\left(\because \lambda_{B}=2 \lambda_{A}\right){/tex}
{tex}u_{B}=\frac{h}{\lambda_{B} \cdot m}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\frac{T_{A}}{T_{B}}=\frac{m u_{A}^{2}}{m u_{B}^{2}}=\frac{4}{1} ; {/tex} Also TA - TB = 1.50
{tex}\therefore{/tex} TB = 0.50
TA =0.50 + 1.50 = 2.00 eV
Also {tex}4.25=h v_{0_{A}}+T_{A}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}h v_{0_{A}}{/tex} = 4.25 - 2.00 = 2.25 eV
{tex}h v_{0_{B}}{/tex} = 4.20 - 0.50 = 3.70 eV

questions.question: If the total energy of an electron in a hydrogen like atom in an excited state is -3.4 eV, then the de Broglie wavelength of the electron is:
questions.explanation:
Total energy {tex}=\frac{-e^{2}}{2 r_{n}}=-3.4 \mathrm{eV}=\frac{E_{1}}{n^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad n^{2}=\frac{-13.6}{-3.4}=4{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} n = 2
The velocity in II orbit {tex}=\frac{u_{1}}{2}=\frac{2.18 \times 10^{8}}{2} \mathrm{cmsec}^{-1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \lambda=\frac{h}{m u}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-27} \times 2}{9.108 \times 10^{-28} \times 2.18 \times 10^{8}}{/tex} = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10

questions.question: An electron is continuously accelerated in vacuum tube under applied potential difference. Calculate the change in kinetic energy if de Broglie wavelength is decreased by 2%.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\lambda \propto \sqrt{\frac{1}{K E}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{K E_{2}}{K E_{1}}}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{K E_{2}}{K E_{1}}=\frac{\lambda_{1}^{2}}{\lambda_{2}^{2}}=\frac{\lambda_{1}^{2}}{\left(0.98 \lambda_{1}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\left[\because \quad \lambda_{2}=\left[\lambda_{1}-\frac{2}{100} \lambda_{2}\right]\right]{/tex}
KE2 = KE1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.04
Change in K.E. {tex}=\left[\frac{K E_{2}-K E_{1}}{K E_{1}}\right] \times 100{/tex}
= 0.04 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 4%

questions.question: The de Broglie wavelength of an electron in the 4th Bohr orbit is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}2 \pi r=n \lambda{/tex} 
r = {tex}\frac{n^{2} a_{0}}{\mathrm{Z}}{/tex}
{tex}2 \pi \times \frac{4^{2}}{1} a_{0}=4 \lambda{/tex}
{tex}\lambda=2 \pi \times \frac{4}{1} a_{0}{/tex} 
{tex}\lambda=8 \pi a_{0}{/tex}

questions.question: At temperature T, the average kinetic energy of any particle is {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}kT. The de Broglie wavelength follows the order:

options.option: Visible photon > Thermal neutron > Thermal electron
Thermal proton > Thermal electron > Visible photon
Visible photon > Thermal electron > Thermal neutron
Thermal proton > Visible photon > Thermal electron

questions.explanation:
The kinetic energy of any particle = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}kT
Also K.E. = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2}=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{kT} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}^{2}=\frac{3 \mathrm{k} \mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{m}}{/tex}
{tex}v=\sqrt{\frac{3 k T}{m}}{/tex}
de-broglie wavelength = {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}=\frac{h}{m \sqrt{\frac{3 k T}{m}}}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{3 \mathrm{kTm}}} ; \lambda \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{m}}}{/tex}
Mass of electron < mass of neutron {tex}\lambda{/tex} (electron) > {tex}\lambda{/tex} (neutron)

questions.question: The de-Broglie wavelength of a particle of mass 6.63 g moving with a velocity of 100 ms-1 is:
questions.explanation:
de Broglie wavelength ({tex}\lambda{/tex}) = {tex}\frac{h}{m v}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}}{6.63 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg} \times 100 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}}{/tex} = 10-33 m

questions.question: The de Broglie wavelength of a car of mass 1000 kg and velocity 36 km/hr is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}{/tex}
h = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 Js
m = 1000 kg
v = 36 km/hr = {tex}\frac{36 \times 10^{3}}{60 \times 60}{/tex} m/sec = 10 m/sec
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{10^{3} \times 10}{/tex} = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-38 m

questions.question: An electron, a proton and an alpha particle have kinetic energies of 16E, 4E and E respectively. What is the qualitative order of their de-Broglie wavelengths?
questions.explanation: de=Broglie wavelength = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m(K 6)}}{/tex}

questions.question: The de Broglie wavelength of a tennis ball of mass 60 g moving with a velocity of 10 metres per second is approximately ________.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{60 \times 10^{-3} \times 10}{/tex}
{tex}\approx{/tex} 10-33 m

questions.question: Calculate the de Broglie wavelength of an electron travelling at 10 % of the speed of light.
questions.explanation:
10 % of the speed of light is
v = {tex}(\frac{10}{100}){/tex} (3.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m s-1)
= 3.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 m s-1
Momentum of the electron (p) = mv
= (9.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg) (3.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 m s-1)
= 27.33 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24 kg m s-1
The de Broglie wavelength of this electron is
{tex}\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{27.33 \times 10^{-24}}{/tex} = 2.42 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 m

questions.question: The de Broglie wavelength of a ball of mass 76 g moving with a velocity of 10 metres per second is ________.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{mv}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{76 \times 10^{-3} \times 10}{/tex}
= 8.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 m

questions.question: The de Broglie wavelength associated with a particle of mass 10-5 kg moving with a velocity of 10 ms-1 is ________.
questions.explanation:
Mass of the particle (m) = 10-5 kg
velocity of the particle (v) = 10 ms-1
{tex}\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}=\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{10^{-5} \times 10}{/tex} = 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-30 m

questions.question:
Which one is the WRONG statement?

  1. de-Broglie’s wavelength is given by {tex}\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}{/tex}, where m = mass of the particle, v = group velocity of the particle.
  2. The uncertainty principle is {tex}\Delta \mathrm{E} \times \Delta \mathrm{t} \geq \frac{\mathrm{h}}{4 \pi}{/tex}.
  3. Half-filled and fully filled orbitals have greater stability due to greater exchange energy, greater symmetry, and a more balanced arrangement.
  4. The energy of 2s orbital is less than the energy of 2p orbital in the case of hydrogen-like atoms.
questions.explanation: The energy of 2s orbital is less than the energy of 2p orbital in case of hydrogen-like atoms.

questions.question: Calculate the wavelength (in nanometer) associated with a proton moving at 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 ms-1 (Mass of proton = 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 kg and h = 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 Js)
questions.explanation:
{tex}\lambda = \frac{h}{mv}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{1.67 \times 10^{-27} \times 1 \times 10^3}{/tex}
= 3.97 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 m {tex}\approx{/tex} 0.40 nm

questions.question: The momentum of a particle having a de-Broglie wavelength of 10-17 m is (given, h = 6.625 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 kg m2/s)
questions.explanation:
According to de-Broglie relation,  {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac hp{/tex}
Given that, h = 6.625 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 kg m2s-1
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17 m
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17 m = {tex}\frac{6.625 \times 10^{34} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^2 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}}{P}{/tex}
p = 6.625 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17 kg ms-1.

questions.question: In an atom, an electron is moving with a speed of 600m/s with an accuracy of 0.005%. The certainty with which the position of the electron can be located is (h = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34Js).
According to the Heisenberg uncertainty principle, in dealing with milligram-sized or heavier objects:

options.option: the associated uncertainties are very important
there are no uncertainties at all
the associated uncertainties are hardly of any real consequence

questions.explanation:
The effect of the Heisenberg uncertainty principle is significant only for motion of microscopic objects, it is negligible in case of macroscopic objects. For example, we apply the concept of uncertainty to an object of mass 1 milligram.
{tex}\Delta x \times \Delta v_x = {h\over 4\pi m}{/tex}
{tex}={ 6.626\times 10^{−34}\over 4\times 3.1416\times 10^{−6}}≈10^{−28}m^2s^{−1}{/tex}
The value of {tex}\Delta x\Delta v_x {/tex}is very small and insignificant in this case. Therefore, when we deal with heavier objects the associated uncertainties have no real consequences and can be neglected.

questions.question: In an atom, an electron is moving with a speed of 600m/s with an accuracy of 0.005%. Certainty with which the position of the electron can be located is (h = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex}10-34 Js)?
questions.explanation:
Velocity = 600m/s
Accuracy = 0.005%
so,uncertainty in the velocity = {tex}{600 \times 5 \over 1000 }={3000\over 1000 }{/tex}= 3 m/s
{tex}∆x {/tex} = ?
Using the Heisenberg uncertainty equation, we have
{tex}Δx \times Δp ={ h\over h\pi }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \Delta x = {h\over 4\pi m \Delta v}{/tex}= {tex}6.634 \times 10^{-34}\over 4 \times 3.14 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31 }\times 3 {/tex} = 1.92 {tex}\times{/tex}10-3m

questions.question: The product of the uncertainties of the exact position and exact momentum (or velocity) of an electron. The product of their uncertainties is always

options.option: equal to or greater than h/2$\pi$
equal to or greater than h/4$\pi$.
equal to or greater than h/3$\pi$
equal to or greater than h/$\pi$

questions.explanation:
The uncertainty principle says that we cannot measure the position (x) and the momentum (p) of a particle with absolute precision. The more accurately we know one of these values, the less accurately we know the other. Multiplying together the errors in the measurements of these values has to give a number greater than or equal to half of a constant called "h-bar". This is equal to Planck's constant (usually written as h) divided by 2{tex}\pi{/tex}.

{tex}Δx. Δp \ge { h\over 4\pi}{/tex}

questions.question: According to Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle, the product of the uncertainties in the position and momentum of an electron is always:

options.option: greater than or equal to h/4$\pi$.
greater than or equal to h/ 6$\pi$
greater than or equal to h/3$\pi$
greater than or equal to h/2$\pi$

questions.explanation:
The uncertainty principle says that we cannot measure the position (x) and the momentum (p) of a particle with absolute precision. The more accurately we know one of these values, the less accurately we know the other. Multiplying together the errors in the measurements of these values (the errors are represented by the triangle symbol in front of each property, the Greek letter "delta") has to give a number greater than or equal to half of a constant called "h-bar". This is equal to Planck's constant (usually written as h) divided by 2{tex}\pi{/tex}.

{tex}Δx. Δp \ge { h\over 4\pi}{/tex}

questions.question: The position of both an electron and helium atom is known within 1.0 nm. The momentum of the electron is known within 5.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-26 kg ms-1. The minimum uncertainty in the measurement of the momentum of the helium atom is
questions.explanation: The product of uncertainties in the position and the momentum of a subatomic particle =h/4{tex}\pi{/tex}. Since Δx is same for electron and helium so Δp must be same for both the particle, i.e,  {tex}5.0 \times {10^{ - 26}}kg{\text{ }}m{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}  (given).

questions.question: “It is impossible to determine simultaneously the position and velocity of small particles such as electron”. It is a statement of
questions.explanation: “It is impossible to determine simultaneously the position and velocity of small particles such as electron”. It is a statement of Heisenberg’s uncertainty principle.

questions.question: Which of the following is responsible to rule out the existence of definite paths or trajectories of electrons?
questions.explanation: Werner Heisenberg, a German physicist in 1927, stated the uncertainty principle which is the consequence of the dual behavior of matter and radiation. It states that it is impossible to determine simultaneously, the exact position and exact momentum (or velocity) of an electron. It rules out the existence of definite paths or trajectories of electron and other similar particles.

questions.question: If uncertainty in position of electron is zero, the uncertainty in its momentum would be:
questions.explanation: {tex}\Delta x \cdot \Delta p \geq \frac{h}{4 \pi}{/tex}, if {tex}\Delta x{/tex} = 0 then {tex}\Delta{/tex}p = {tex}\infty{/tex}

questions.question: A ball of mass 200 g is moving with a velocity of 10m sec-1. If the error in measurement of velocity is 0.1 %, the uncertainty in its position is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta u=\frac{0.1}{100} \times 10=10^{-2} \mathrm{~m} \sec ^{-1}{/tex}, Now {tex}\Delta u \cdot \Delta X=\frac{h}{4 \pi m}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}\Delta X=\frac{6.625 \times 10^{-34}}{4 \times 10^{-2} \times 3.14 \times 200 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 2.64 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-32 m

questions.question: Uncertainty in position and momentum are equal. Uncertainty in velocity is:
questions.explanation:
Given {tex}\Delta p=\Delta X{/tex} also {tex}\Delta X \cdot \Delta p=\frac{h}{4 \pi}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta p=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{h}{\pi}}{/tex}
or {tex}m \cdot \Delta u=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{h}{\pi}}{/tex}
or {tex}\Delta u=\frac{1}{2 m} \sqrt{\frac{h}{\pi}}{/tex}

questions.question: The uncertainty principle and the concept of wave nature of matter was proposed by ... and ... respectively.
questions.explanation: Heisenberg’s uncertainly principle; de Broglie’s dual concept.

questions.question: The uncertainty in position of a minute particle of mass 25 g in space is 10-5m. The uncertainty in its velocity (in ms-1) is:
questions.explanation: {tex}\Delta u=\frac{h}{4 \pi m \cdot \Delta x}=\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{4 \times 3.14 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-5}}{/tex}=2.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-28 m/s

questions.question:
Uncertainty in the position of an electron (mass = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg) moving with a velocity 300 ms-1, accurate upto 0.001 %, will be:
[h =6.63 {tex}\times {/tex} 10-34 js]
questions.explanation:
According to Heisenberg's uncertainty principle Δx×m×ΔV=h/4{tex}\pi{/tex}​
Δx=  uncertainty in the position
m=9.1×10−31kg=  mass of electron
ΔV=300×.001​/1000=0.003m/s 
uncertainty in the velocity
h=6.62×10−36Js= Planck's constant
Substitute values in the above formula
Δx×9.1×10−31×0.003 = 6.62×10−34​/4×3.1416
Δx=1.92×10−2m

questions.question:
In an atom, an electron is moving with a speed of 600 m/s with an accuracy of 0.005%. Certainity with which the position of the electron can be located is :
(h = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 kg m2s-1, mass of electron, {tex}e_{m}=9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}{/tex})
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta u \cdot \Delta X=\frac{h}{4 \pi m}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \Delta X=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{4 \times 3.14 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 600 \times \frac{0.005}{100}}{/tex}
1.92 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m

questions.question: Uncertainty in position of an electron (mass = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex}10-28 g) moving with a velocity of 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 cm/s accurate upto 0.001% will be (Use {tex}\frac{h}{4 \pi}{/tex}) in uncertainty expression where h - 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 erg-second)
questions.explanation:
According to Heisenberg's uncertainty principle,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}x{tex}\cdot{/tex}{tex}\Delta{/tex}p = {tex}\frac{h}{4 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}x {tex}\cdot{/tex}{tex}\Delta{/tex}(m.v) = {tex}\frac{h}{4 \pi}{/tex}
Given that m = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-28 g
{tex}\Delta{/tex}v = 0.001% of 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 104
= {tex}\frac{0.001}{100}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 = 0.3 cm/s
h = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17 ergs
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ({tex}\Delta{/tex}x) (9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-28 g) (0.3 cm/s)
= {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{erg} \mathrm{s}}{4 \times 3.14}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}x = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{erg} \mathrm{s}}{4 \times 3.14 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-28} \mathrm{~g} \times 0.3 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}x = 1.93 cm.

questions.question: The position of both, an electron and a helium atom is known within 1.0 nm. Further the momentum of the electron is known within 5.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-26 kgms-1. The minimum uncertainty in the measurement of the momentum of the helium atom is :
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta p \cdot \Delta x=\frac{h}{4 \pi}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \Delta p=\frac{h}{4 \pi \cdot \Delta x}=\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{4 \times 3.14 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex}
{tex}=5.275 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}{/tex}

questions.question: The uncertainty in the momentum of an electron is 10-5 kg ms-1.The uncertainty in its position will be:
questions.explanation: Use {tex}\Delta x \cdot \Delta p=\frac{h}{4 \pi} {/tex}

questions.question: Given : The mass of electron is 9.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg, Planck constant is 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 Js, the uncertainty involved in the measurement of velocity within a distance of 0.1 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta u \cdot \Delta x=\frac{h}{4 \pi m}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta u=\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{4 \times 3.14 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 0.1 \times 10^{-10}}=5.8 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}{/tex}

questions.question: The measurement of the electron position is associated with an uncertainty in momentum, which is equal to 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18g cm s-1. The uncertainty in electron velocity is: (mass of an electron is 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-28g).
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta x \cdot \Delta u=\frac{h}{4 \pi m}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta p=1 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm} \sec ^{-1}{/tex}
{tex}m \times \Delta u=1 \times 10^{-18}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta u=\frac{1 \times 10^{-18}}{9 \times 10^{-28}}=1.1 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~cm} \sec ^{-1}{/tex}

questions.question: If uncertainty in position and momentum are equal, then uncertainty in velocity is :
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta x=\Delta p{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \Delta x \cdot \Delta p=\frac{h}{4 \pi}{/tex} 
or {tex}\Delta x=\sqrt{\frac{h}{4 \pi}}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\Delta x \cdot \Delta u=\frac{h}{4 \pi m}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta u=\frac{h}{4 \pi m} \times \sqrt{\frac{4 \pi}{h}}=\frac{1}{2 m} \times \sqrt{\frac{h}{\pi}}{/tex}

questions.question:
Which one is the wrong statement?

  1. de Broglie’s wavelength is given by {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}{/tex}, where m = mass of the particle, v = group velocity of the particle.
  2. The uncertainty principle is {tex}\Delta E \times \Delta t \geq \frac{h}{4 \pi}{/tex}
  3. Half-filled and fully-filled orbitals have greater stability due to greater exchange energy, greater symmetry and more balanced arrangement
  4. The energy of 2s orbital is less than the energy of 2p orbital in case of Hydrogen like atoms.
questions.explanation: For a single electronic species like H, He+, Li2+ etc. energy depends on value of n only i.e., E of 2s = 2p

questions.question: Which of the following are correct about the Heisenberg uncertainty principle where all terms have their usual meanings?
questions.explanation:
According to Heisenberg principle
{tex}\Delta x \cdot \Delta p \geq \frac{h}{4 \pi}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\Delta x \cdot m \Delta u \geq \frac{h}{4 \pi}{/tex} ....(ii)
{tex}\therefore \Delta x \cdot \Delta u \geq \frac{h}{4 \pi m}{/tex} ...(iii)
Also, {tex}\Delta E=m \Delta u^{2}=m\left[\frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}\right]^{2}{/tex} ..(iv)
From (ii) {tex}\Delta x \cdot m \cdot \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t} \geq \frac{h}{4 \pi}{/tex} ....(v)
By (iv) and (v)
{tex}\therefore \quad \Delta E \cdot \Delta t \geq \frac{h}{4 \pi}{/tex}

questions.question: Table-tennis ball has a mass 10 g and a speed of 90 m/s. If speed can be measured within an accuracy of 4% what will be the uncertainty in speed and position
questions.explanation:
Uncertainty in the speed of ball
= {tex}\frac{90 \times 4}{100}=\frac{360}{100}{/tex} = 3.6 ms-1
Uncertainty in position = {tex}\frac{h}{4 \pi m \Delta V}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} J S}{4 \times 3.14 \times 10 \times 10^{-3} k g g^{-1} \times 3.6 m s^{-1}}{/tex}
= 1.46 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-33 m

questions.question:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are CORRECT about the uncertainty principle?

  1. It rules out the existence of definite paths or trajectories of electrons and other similar particles.
  2. It is significant only for motion of macroscopic objects and is negligible for that of microscopic objects.
  3. The precise statements of the position and momentum of electrons have to be replaced by the statements of probability.
A ball of mass 15 g is moving at a speed of 80 cm s-1 measured within an accuracy of 3.5 %. What will be the uncertainty in the position of the ball?
questions.explanation:
The uncertainty in the speed of the ball will be,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}v = {tex}\frac{3.5}{100}\times{/tex} 80
= 2.8 cm s-1
Uncertainty in a position of the ball is
{tex}\Delta x=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{4 \pi \mathrm{m} \Delta \mathrm{v}}=\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-27}}{4 \times 3.14 \times 15 \times 2.8}{/tex}
= 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-29 cm

questions.question: The position of both an electron and a helium atom is known within 1.0 nm. If the momentum of the electron is known within 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-26 kg m s-1, the minimum uncertainty in the measurement of the momentum of the helium atom is ________.
questions.explanation: The product of uncertainty in the position and the momentum of a sub-atomic particle = {tex}\frac{h}{4\pi}{/tex} (i.e., a constant). Since {tex}\Delta{/tex}x is the same for the given electron and the given helium atom, {tex}\Delta{/tex}p must be the same for both the particles i.e., 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-26 kg m s-1 (given).

questions.question: If uncertainty in the position of a particle ({tex}\Delta{/tex}x) is ________, the uncertainty in momentum ({tex}\Delta{/tex}p) would be ________.
questions.explanation: If the position of a particle is measured accurately, there will be more error in the measurement of its momentum.

questions.question: According to Heisenberg’s uncertainty principle, the product of uncertainty in position and velocity for an electron is ________. [Given that mass of electron = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg Planck's constant = 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 kg m2s-1]
questions.explanation:
By using {tex}\Delta{/tex}x {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}p = {tex}\frac{h}{4\pi}{/tex}; {tex}\Delta{/tex}x {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}v {tex}\times{/tex} m = {tex}\frac{h}{4\pi}{/tex}
where; {tex}\Delta{/tex}x = uncertainty in position
{tex}\Delta{/tex}v = uncertainty in velocity
{tex}\Delta{/tex}x {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}v = {tex}\frac{h}{4\pi\times m}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{4 \times 3.14 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31}}{/tex}
= 5.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 m2s-1

questions.question: The uncertainty in momentum of an electron is 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 kg m/s. The uncertainty in its position will be: (Given h = 6.62 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 kg m2/s).
questions.explanation:
According to Heisenberg'S uncertainty principle,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}x{tex}\cdot{/tex}{tex}\Delta{/tex}p = {tex}\frac{h}{4 \pi}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}p = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 kg m/s; {tex}\Delta{/tex}x = ?
{tex}\Delta{/tex}x{tex}\cdot{/tex}(1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 kg m/s) = {tex}\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^2 / \mathrm{s}}{4 \times 3.14}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}x = {tex}\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-29}}{4 \times 3.14} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}x = 5.27 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-30 m.

questions.question:
Statement I: According to Bohr's model of hydrogen atom, the angular momentum of an electron in a given stationary state is quantised.
Statement II: The concept of electron in Bohr's orbit violates the Heisenberg uncertainty principle.

options.option: Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct

questions.explanation: According to Bohr's model of hydrogen atom the angular momentum of an electron in a given stationary state is quantized and is equal to {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}. Also the concept of electron in Bohr's orbit violates the Heisenberg uncertainty principle. This is because according to Bohr's theory electrons revolve around the nucleus in fixed circular paths called orbits, having fixed energies whereas Heisenberg uncertainty principle states that the position and momentum of an electron can never be measured simultaneously, thus both the statements are correct.

questions.question: Find the energy of the photons which correspond to light of frequency 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 Hz (Hint: h = Planck’s constant = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 Js)?
questions.topicName: Quantum Mechanical Model of Atom and its Features
questions.explanation:
We know Planck's equation is
E = hv
where E is energy, h is Planck’s constant and v is frequency.
E = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 = 1.988 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 J

questions.question: Calculate the energy required for the process He+(g) {tex}\to{/tex} He2+(g) + e- (Hint: The ionization energy of the lowest state, also called as the ground state is 2.18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 J atom-1).
questions.topicName: Quantum Mechanical Model of Atom and its Features
questions.explanation:
He+ ion is a single electron species which resembles like hydrogen. Therefore, the energies of the stationary states of hydrogen-like ions are given by the expression
{tex}E_n ={ 2.18 \times 10^{-18} }\times { Z^2 \over n^2 }{/tex}
Here n = 1 and Z = 2
{tex}E_n = {2.18 \times 10^{-18}}\times {2^2\over 1^2}{/tex} = 8.72 {tex}\times{/tex} 10- 18J atom-1

questions.question: Isotopes of an element have ________.

options.option: Similar physical but different chemical properties
Similar chemical but different physical properties

questions.topicName: Quantum Mechanical Model of Atom and its Features
questions.explanation:
• Isotopes are atoms of the same element that have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.

  • Because chemical properties depend on the electron configuration, which is determined by the number of protons (and thus electrons), isotopes have similar chemical properties.

  • Physical properties (like mass, density, boiling point) are affected by the atomic mass, which varies between isotopes due to the different number of neutrons.

questions.question:
Arrange the following orbitals in decreasing order of energy?

  1. n = 3, l = 0, m = 0
  2. n = 4, l = 0, m = 0
  3. n = 3, l = 1, m = 0
  4. n = 3, l = 2, m = 1
questions.topicName: Quantum Mechanical Model of Atom and its Features
questions.explanation:
1. n = 3; l = 0; n + l = 3
  2. n = 4; l = 0; n + l = 4
  3. n = 3; l = 1; n + l = 4
  4. n = 3; l = 2; n + l = 5

As per Hund’s rule energy is given by (n + l) value. If value of (n + /) remains same then energy is given by n only. Therefore the order of energy is: D > B > C > A

The wave function {tex}(\Psi){/tex} of 2s is given by
{tex}\Psi_{2s}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2} \pi}\left(\frac{1}{a_0}\right)^{1 / 2}\left(2-\frac{r}{a_0}\right) e^{-r / 2 a_0}{/tex}
At r = r0, radial node is formed. Thus, r0 in terms of a0

At note {tex}\Psi_{2 s}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}2-\frac{r_0}{a_0}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r0 = 2a0

questions.question:
Given below are the quantum numbers for 4 electrons.

  1. n = 3, l = 2, m1 = 1, ms = {tex}+ \frac 12{/tex}
  2. n = 4, l = 1, m1 = 0, ms = {tex}+ \frac 12{/tex}
  3. n = 4, l = 2, m1 = -2, ms = {tex}- \frac 12{/tex}
  4. n = 3, l = 1, m1 = -1, ms = {tex}+ \frac 12{/tex}
questions.topicName: Quantum Mechanical Model of Atom and its Features
questions.explanation:
Lower the value of (n + 1) for an orbital, lower is its energy. If two orbitals have same value of (n + l) then lower value of n will have the lower energy
A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3d {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n + l = 3 + 2 = 5
B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4p {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n + l = 4 + 1 = 5
C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4d {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n + l = 4 + 2 = 6
D {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3s {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (n + l) = 3 + 0 = 3
order is D < A < B < C

questions.question:
The energy of one mole of photons of radiation of wavelength 300 nm is
[Given: h = 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 Js, NA = 6.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023mol-1, c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 ms-1]

Energy of one mole of photons = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda} \times N_{\mathrm{A}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{300 \times 10^{-9}} \times 6.02 \times 10^{23}{/tex}
= 399.13 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 Joule\mole = 399 kJ\mole

questions.explanation: Orbitals with subshell quantum number l = 0 are called s orbitals. All s orbitals are spherical in shape and have spherical symmetry. This means that the wave function will depend only on the distance from the nucleus and not on the direction.

questions.question:
The following transition occurs when Lithium atoms are sprayed into hot flame. The various steps are numbered for identification.
{tex}2s\xrightarrow{I}2p\xrightarrow{{II}}3d{/tex}{tex}\xrightarrow{{III}}3p\xrightarrow{{IV}}4s\xrightarrow{V}3P{/tex}
Which of these transitions result in the emission of light?
questions.explanation:
{tex}2s\xrightarrow{I}2p\xrightarrow{{II}}3d{/tex}{tex}\xrightarrow{{III}}3p\xrightarrow{{IV}}4s\xrightarrow{V}3P{/tex}
The energy of 3d is more than 3p therefore it will emit energy, similarly from 4s {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 3p transition.

v = 1368 kHz = 1368 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 s-1
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{c}{v}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}}{1368 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}}{/tex} = 219.3 m

questions.question: Usings s,p,d, and f notation, choose the orbital with n=4, l=3 quantum numbers.
questions.explanation: Here n= principal quantum number, l= azimuthal quantum number. For n = 4 and l = 3 The orbital is 4f (can have a maximum of 14 electrons).

questions.question: Which of the following sets of quantum numbers represents the highest energy of an atom?
According to the quantum theoretical model of an atom, each orbital is designated by three quantum numbers labelled as n, l, and ml. The values these can take are:
Maximum number of electrons in a subshell with l = 3 and n = 4 is _____.
questions.explanation:
Here l = 3 means the subshell is f. 
n = 4 means it is present in the 4th orbit. Finally, it is a 4f subshell.
The number of orbital in f sub shell = 2l + 1 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 + 1 = 7
Each orbital can accommodate 2 electrons = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 14 electrons.
Therefore, the maximum number of electrons in a subshell with l = 3 and n = 4 (4f) is 14 electrons.

questions.question: The orbital with quantum numbers n = 4, l = 3 is
questions.explanation: For n = 4 the possible values of l are  0,1, 2, 3. The orbital with l = 3 is f orbital.

questions.question: Which of the following sets of quantum numbers represent the highest energy of an electron?
questions.explanation: In a multi electron atom the energy of an electron is determined by its n+l value, higher the value, higher is the energy.

questions.question: The orbital with n = 3 and l = 2 is
questions.explanation:
For n = 3 the possible  values of l are l = 0, the s orbital
l = 1, the p orbital and l = 2, the d orbital

questions.question: In an atom, the maximum number of electrons in an orbit / principal energy level n is
questions.explanation: Since the maximum number of electrons in each orbital is 2, the maximum number of electrons in an entire quantum level is 2n2.

questions.question: For azimuthal quantum number (l)  = 2,  the values of magnetic quantum number will be:
questions.explanation:
The magnetic quantum number gives the number of  orbitals in a particular subshell within a principal energy level.
The possible values range from  +l  through 0 to -l , a total of  2l + 1 values.

questions.question:
The quantum number of four electrons are given below:

  1. n = 4, l = 2,ml = - 2, ms = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
  2. n = 3,l = 2, ml = 1, ms = {tex}+\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
  3. n = 4, l = 1,ml = 0, ms = {tex}+\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
  4. n = 3, l = 1,ml = 1, ms = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

The correct order of their increasing energies will be
questions.explanation:
Smaller the value of (n + l), smaller the energy. If two or more sub-orbits have same values of (n + l), sub-orbits with lower values of n has lower energy. The (n + l) values of the given options are as follows :
I. n = 4, l = 2; n + l = 6
II. n = 3, l = 2; n + l = 5
III. n = 4, l = 1, n + 7 = 5
IV. n = 3, l = 1, n + l = 4
Among II and III, n = 3 has lower value of energy. Thus, the correct order of their increasing energies will be
IV < II < III < I

questions.question: The correct set of four quantum numbers for the valence electrons of rubidium atom (Z = 37) is
questions.explanation:
Given, atomic number of Rb, Z = 37
Thus, its electronic configuration is [Kr] 5s1. Since, the last electron or valence electron enter in 5s subshell.
So, the quantum numbers are n = 5, 1 - 0, (for 5-orbital) m = 0
({tex}\because{/tex} m = +1 to -1), 5 = + 1/2 o r-1/2.

Cl (17) = Is2 2s2 2p6 3s2 3p5

The last, unpaired electron has, n = 3, l = 1(p) and m can have any of the three value (-1, 0, + 1).

questions.question: The principal quantum number of an atom is related to the

options.option: orientation of the orbital in space

questions.explanation: The principal quantum number 'n' represents orbit number hence, determine the size of orbitals.

questions.question: The maximum number of electrons, present in a sub-orbit that is represented by Azimuthal quantum number (/) = 3, will be
questions.explanation: The maximum number of electrons in l=3 that is an f-orbital would be 14. This is because for a given value of l there are (2l+1) values of ml and each ml contains 2 electrons. Therefore multiply the 7 by 2.

questions.question: The total number of orbitals in a shell can be given in terms of n if n is principal quantum number
questions.explanation:
Given principal quantum number(n) of the shell = n
Number of orbitals in a shell is given by n2
So the number of orbitals in the given shell = n2.

questions.question: Principal quantum number of an atom is related to the

options.option: Orientation of the orbital in space

questions.explanation:
The principal quantum number 'n' represents orbit number hence, determine the size of orbitals. The principal quantum numbers are represented by the letters 'K, L, M, N' or equal to 1, 2, 3, 4, etc.
The maximum number of electrons that can accommodate in a principal energy shell is given by 2n2.

questions.question: Principal, azimuthal and magnetic quantum numbers are respectively related to
questions.explanation:
• Principal, azimuthal and magnetic quantum numbers are respectively related to size, shape, and orientation.
  • The principal quantum number gives the average distance of the electron from the nucleus. It corresponds to the principal energy level to which the electron belongs.
  • The azimuthal quantum number denotes the subshell (orbital) to which the electron belongs and also determines the shape of the orbital and the energy associated with the angular momentum of the electron.
  • The magnetic quantum number determines the preferred orientations of orbitals in space.

questions.question: Azimuthal quantum number defines
questions.explanation: An azimuthal quantum number is a quantum number for an atomic orbital that determines its orbital angular momentum and describes the shape of the orbital. The azimuthal quantum number is the second of a set of quantum numbers which describe the unique quantum state of an electron It is also known as the orbital angular momentum quantum number, orbital quantum number or second quantum number, and is symbolized as l.

questions.question: The quantum number m of a free gaseous atom is associated with

options.option: The energy of the orbital in the absence of a magnetic field
The effective volume of the orbital
The spatial orientation of the orbital

questions.explanation:
The quantum number, m of a free gaseous atom is associated with the spatial orientation of an orbital.
The magnetic quantum number is also called orientation quantum number. It gives the distribution of electron clouds in space around the nucleus.
For example, the value of m for p orbitals are 1, 0 and -1. p subshell contains 3 orbitals px​, py,​ and pz​ .
They are oriented along x, y, and z axis respectively. Different orientations of p orbitals correspond to different values of the magnetic quantum number, m.

questions.question: For principal quantum number n = 4. The total number of orbitals having / = 3 is
questions.explanation: Each subshell of quantum number l contains 2l+1 orbitals. Thus, for l=3, there are (2×3)+1=7orbitals.

questions.question: Orbital angular momentum depends on ________.
questions.explanation: Azimuthal quantum number. 'I' also known as orbital angular momentum or subsidiary quantum number. It defines the three-dimensional shape of the orbital. For a given value of n,1 can have n values ranging from 0 to n-1, that is, for a given value of n, the possible values of 1 are: 1 = 0, 1, 2, .......(n - 1).

questions.question: Total number of orbitals associated with third shell will be:
questions.explanation: No. of orbitals in 3rd shell = n2 = 32 = 9

questions.question: Orbital angular momentum depends on:
questions.explanation: Angular momentum = {tex}\frac{l}{2 \pi} \sqrt{l(l+1)}{/tex}

questions.question: The quantum numbers for the last electron in an atom are n = 3, I = 1 and m = -1. The atom is:
questions.explanation: It is 3px or 3p, orbital, i.e., Al having 3s23 p1 configuration.

can be placed in order of increasing energy as :

l = 1, ml = - 1, 0, +1

questions.question: Consider the hypothetical situation where the azimuthal quantum number, l, takes values 0, 1, 2, .... n + 1, where n is the principal quantum number. Then, the element with atomic number:
questions.explanation:
Under the given situation for
n = 1, l = 0, 1, 2
n = 2, l = 0, 1, 2, 3
n = 3, l = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
According to (n + 1) rule of order of filling of subshells will be:
1s 1p 1d 2s 2p 3s 2d 3f
Atomic number 6 - 1s2 1p4
Atomic number 9 - 1s2 1p6 1d1
Atomic number 8 - 1s2 1p6
Atomic number 13 - 1s2 1p6 1d5
Therefore option (13 has a half-filled valence subshell) is correct. An atomic number of the first noble gas will be 18 (1s2 1p6 1d10).

questions.explanation: The principal quantum number (n) indicates the energy level and size of an electron's orbit. Higher n values mean electrons are farther from the nucleus and have more energy. It does not describe shape, spin, or orientation.

l = 1, m = -1

Hence there are 13 of 14 electrons as in d-orbital it may be one or two-electron having m = -2

questions.explanation:
n = 4, l = 0, me = 0
l = 2; me = -1, 0, +1

l = 3; me = -3, -2, -1, 0, +1, +2, +3
There are three orbitals having me = +1 thus maximum number of electrons in them will be 6.

{tex}\therefore{/tex} For d-orbital, l = 2

questions.question: The number of angular nodes and radial nodes in 3s orbital are
questions.explanation:
0 angular nodes and 2 radial nodes.
No. of angular nodes = I
No. of Radial nodes = n - l - 1
Given for 3s orbital n = 3, I = 0

No. of radial nodes = 2.

questions.question:
Consider the following sets of quantum numbers:

S.No. n l m s
(i) 3 0 0 + {tex}\frac12{/tex}
(ii) 2 2 1 + {tex}\frac12{/tex}
(iii) 4 3 -2 - {tex}\frac12{/tex}
(iv) 1 0 -1 - {tex}\frac12{/tex}
(v) 3 2 3 + {tex}\frac12{/tex}

Which of the following sets of quantum numbers is not possible?
questions.explanation:
For the given value of n, the possible values of l are 0 to n - 1. For the given value of l, the permissible value of ml = +l to -I. Also for each ml there are two values of s, i.e., + {tex}\frac12{/tex} and - {tex}\frac12{/tex}.

  1. For n = 3, possible values of l = 0, 1, 2. Now, for l = 0, value of m = 0 and 5 can be + {tex}\frac12{/tex} or -{tex}\frac12{/tex}. Therefore, the given set of quantum numbers is correct.
  2. For n = 2, possible values of l = 0, 1. Therefore, l cannot have value = 2. Thus, this set of quantum numbers is wrong.
  3. For n = 4, possible values of l are 0, I, 2 and 3. For l = 3, values of m = +3, +2, + 1, 0, -1, -2, -3. Also s can have value of + {tex}\frac12{/tex} or - {tex}\frac12{/tex}. Hence, the given set of quantum numbers is possible.
  4. For n = 1, the only possible value of l = 0. Also, m = 0 is applicable for l = 0 and m = -1 is not a valid one. Thus, this set of quantum numbers is not valid.
  5. For n = 3, the possible values of l are 0, 1 and 2. Now for l = 2, values of m are +2, + 1, 0, -1 and-2 only. So m = +3 is not valid. Thus, the given set of quantum numbers is not possible.
    Hence, the sets (ii), (iv) and (v) are impossible sets of quantum numbers.

questions.question: Which of the following configuration is correct for iron?
questions.explanation: Atomic number of iron (Fe) is 26. These 26 electrons are filled in the order of increasing energy of orbitals, i.e., lower energy orbital gets filled before than the higher energy orbitals. Only in the option (1s2, 2s2 2p6, 3s2 3p6 3d6, 4s2) the number of electrons is 26 and the orbitals are in increasing order of their energies hence, it is the correct configuration for iron.

questions.question: The orbitals are called degenerate when:

options.option: they have the same wave functions but different energies
they have the same wave functions
they have different wave functions but same energy

questions.explanation: The orbitals with same energy but having different orientation in space are said to be degenerate orbitals. For example, in 2p subshell there are three degenerate orbitals viz, Px' Py and pz. They all have same energy but oriented towards different axes in space.

questions.question: If an electron has spin quantum number +{tex}\frac12{/tex} and magnetic quantum number -1, it cannot be present in:
questions.explanation:
For s - orbital, l is 0. Also, for l = 0, m (magnetic quantum number) can have only value of 0. While 
d - orbital (l = 2) can have m = +2, +1, 0, -1, -2; 
f - orbital (l = 3) can have m = +3, +2, +1, 0, -1, -2, -3;
p - orbital, (l = 1) can have m = + 1, 0, -1. Thus, only for s - orbital, m cannot have value of -1 though s can have value of + {tex}\frac12{/tex} or - {tex}\frac12{/tex} in all these orbitals.

questions.question: The total number of electrons that can be accommodated in all the orbitals having principal quantum number 2 and azimuthal quantum number 1 are:
questions.explanation: Azimuthal quantum number (l) = 1 corresponds to p-subshell and for principal quantum number (n) = 2 the subshell is 2p. Now, p - subshell has 3 orbitals and each orbital contains maximum of 2 electrons. Thus, the total number of electrons it can accommodate is 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 6.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements are correct?

  1. The electronic configuration of Cr is (Ar) {tex}3 d^{5} 4 s^{1}{/tex}.
  2. The magnetic quantum number may have a negative value.
  3. In the ground state of an atom, the orbitals are filled in order of their increasing energies.
  4. The total number of nodes are given by {tex}n-2{/tex}.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
questions.explanation:
1. The electronic configuration of Cr is {tex}[{Ar}] 3 d^{5} 4 s^{1}{/tex} is correct.
  2. The magnetic quantum number may have a negative value because it depends upon azimuthal quantum number so, {tex}m=-l{/tex} to {tex}+l{/tex} is correct.
  3. According to Aufbau principle, the ground state of an atom, the orbitals are filled in order of their increasing order of their degenerate energy levels so, this statement is also correct.
  4. The total number of nodes are given by {tex}n-1{/tex} so, this statement is incorrect.

topics.topicName: Shapes of s, p and d orbitals

questions.question: The number of radial nodes for 3p orbital is __________.
questions.topicName: Shapes of s, p and d orbitals
questions.explanation:
Number of radial nodes = n−l−1
where n = principal quantum number, l = azimuthal quantum number
For 3p orbital, n=3 and l=1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The number of radial nodes for 3p orbital =3−1−1=3−2=1.

questions.question: The number of radial nodes in 3s and 2p respectively are
questions.topicName: Shapes of s, p and d orbitals
questions.explanation:
The number of radial nodes is given by expression (n - 1 - 1)
For 3s, number of nodes = 3 - 0 - l = 2
For 2p, number of nodes = 2 - 1 -1 = 0

questions.question: The number of nodal planes in a px orbital is
questions.topicName: Shapes of s, p and d orbitals
questions.explanation: Nodal plane is an imaginary plane on which probability of finding an electron is minimum. Every p-orbital has one nodal plane :

questions.topicName: Shapes of s, p and d orbitals
questions.explanation: At the radial node, the value of Ψ2 is zero.

questions.question: The shape of the orbital with the value of l = 2, m = 0 is
questions.topicName: Shapes of s, p and d orbitals
questions.explanation: l = 2, m = 0 refers to {tex}3d_z^2 {/tex} orbital by conventions.

questions.question: The number of radial nodes for 3p orbital is ________.
questions.topicName: Shapes of s, p and d orbitals
questions.explanation:
Explanation: For p orbital, n = 3 and l = 1
No. of radial nodes = n - l - 1
No. of radial nodes for 3p orbital = 3 - 1 - 1 = 1

questions.question: Number of angular nodes for 4d orbital is ________.

questions.question:
The electrons, identified by quantum numbers n and l, can be placed in order of increasing energy, from the lowest to highest, as

  1. n = 4, l = 1,
  2. n = 4, l = 0,
  3. n = 3, l = 2,
  4. n = 3, l = 1
questions.topicName: Rules for filling electrons in orbitals - Aufbau principle
questions.explanation:
1. n = 4, l = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 p-orbital
  2. n = 4, l = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 s-orbital
  3. n = 3, l = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 d-orbital
  4. n = 3, l = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 d-orbital

According to Aufbau principle, energies of above mentioned orbitals are in the order of
(iv) 3p < (ii) 4s < (iii) 3d < (i) 4p

questions.question: If n = 6, the correct sequence for filling of electrons will be :
questions.topicName: Rules for filling electrons in orbitals - Aufbau principle
questions.explanation:
For n=6n = 6n=6, the electron filling follows the order based on increasing energy levels, as described by the Aufbau principle. Let's break down the options:

  • For n=6n = 6n=6:
    • The 6s6s6s orbital will be filled first.
    • After that, the 4f4f4f orbital (since n−2=4n-2 = 4n−2=4) is filled.
    • Next, the 5d5d5d orbital (since n−1=5n-1 = 5n−1=5) is filled.
    • Finally, the 6p6p6p orbital is filled.

So, the correct sequence is:

6s→4f→5d→6p6s {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 4f {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 5d {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 6p6s→4f→5d→6p

questions.question: 4d, 5p, 5f and 6p orbitals are arranged in the order of decreasing energy. The correct option is:
questions.topicName: Rules for filling electrons in orbitals - Aufbau principle
questions.explanation:
(n + 1) values for, 4d = 4 + 2 = 6
5p = 5 + 1 = 6
5f = 5 + 3 = 8
6p = 6 + 1 = 7
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Correct order of energy would be
5f > 6p > 5p > 4d

questions.question: If the principal quantum number n = 6, the correct sequence of filling of electrons will be:
questions.topicName: Rules for filling electrons in orbitals - Aufbau principle
questions.explanation:
According to Aufbau principle, the sequence of filling electrons in sixth period is
6s - 4f - 5d - 6p i.e., (ns) {tex}\to{/tex} (n - 2)f {tex}\to{/tex} (n - 1)d {tex}\to{/tex} np

questions.question: In the sixth period, the orbitals that are filled are:

questions.topicName: Pauli's Exclusion Principle and Hund's Rule
questions.explanation: The Pauli Exclusion Principle states that, in an atom or molecule, no two electrons can have the same four electronic quantum numbers. As an orbital can contain a maximum of only two electrons, the two electrons must have opposing spins.

questions.explanation: As per Pauli’s exclusion principle “no two electrons in the same atom can have all the four quantum numbers equal or an orbital cannot contain more than two electrons and it can accommodate two electrons only when their directions of spins are opposite.”

questions.question: Pairing of electrons in the orbitals belonging to the same subshell (p, d or f) does not take place until each orbital belonging to that subshell is singly occupied. This is called
questions.topicName: Pauli's Exclusion Principle and Hund's Rule
questions.explanation: Pairing of electrons in the orbitals belonging to the same subshell (p, d or f) does not take place until each orbital belonging to that subshell has got one electron each, i.e. it is singly occupied. This is called Hund’s rule of maximum multiplicity.

questions.question: The manganese (Z=25) has the outer configuration:
questions.explanation:
3d54s2
The 3d orbital in this case is exactly half-filled and the 4s orbital is completely filled. The half filled and completely filled electronic configurations have a symmetrical distribution of electrons which leads to stability. The 4s sub-energy level is at lower energy than the 3d sub-energy level.

questions.explanation:

The above configuration is exception to Aufbau’s principle.

questions.question: Which of the following has maximum energy?
questions.explanation: Electrons fill low energy orbitals (closer to the nucleus) before they fill higher energy ones. Where there is a choice between orbitals of equal energy, they fill the orbitals singly as far as possible. 3d orbital has higher energy electrons than 3p and 3s. In the given option it has the highest number of electrons, there are two unpaired electrons in 3d orbital while 3p has three unpaired electrons. Therefore has the maximum energy.

questions.question: Which of the following does not have valence electron in 3d-subshell?
questions.explanation:
P(0): Phosphorus is not a d- block element. It is an element from p-block (15th group or Nitrogen group), thus, it can not have any valance electron in d subshell.
All other elements are d-block elements, therefore has valance electron in 3d subshell.
Electronic configuration of Cr(I) is 1s22s22p63s23p63d5.
Electronic configuration of Fe(III) is1s22s22p63s23p63d5.
Electronic configuration of Mn(II) is 1s22s22p63s23p63d5.
So, all these have valence electrons in 3d−subshell.

questions.question: Which of the following options does not represent ground state electronic configuration of an atom?
questions.explanation:
Correct electronic configuration of the two subshells (3d and 4s) should be 3 d104s1
In certain elements such as Cu or Cr, where the two subshells (4s and 3d) differ slightly in their energies, an electron shifts from a subshell of lower energy (4s) to a subshell of higher energy (3d), provided such a shift results in all orbitals of the subshell of higher energy getting either completely filled or half-filled. Therefore it is wrong.

questions.question: The pair of ions having same electronic configuration is ________.

questions.question:
Match the metal ions given in Column I with the spin magnetic moments of the ions given in Column II and assign the correct code :

Column I Column II
1. {tex}\mathrm{Co}^{3+}{/tex} (i) {tex}\sqrt{8}{/tex}BM
2. {tex}\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}{/tex} (ii) {tex}\sqrt{35}{/tex}BM
3. {tex}\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}{/tex} (iii) {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}BM
4. Ni2+ (iv) {tex}\sqrt{24 }{/tex}BM
  (v) {tex}\sqrt{15}{/tex}BM
questions.explanation:
Co3+ = [Ar]3d6, Unpaired electron (n) = 4
Spin magnetic moment = {tex}\sqrt{4(4+2)}=\sqrt{24} \mathrm{BM}{/tex}
Cr3+ = [Ar]3d3, Unpaired electron (n) = 3
Spin magnetic moment = {tex}\sqrt{3(3+2)}=\sqrt{15} \mathrm{BM}{/tex}
Fe3+ = [Ar]3d5, Unpaired electron (n) = 5
Spin magnetic moment = {tex}\sqrt{5(5+2)}=\sqrt{35} \mathrm{BM}{/tex}
Ni2+ = [Ar]3d8, Unpaired electron (n) = 2
Spin magnetic moment = {tex}\sqrt{2(2+2)}=\sqrt{8} \mathrm{BM}{/tex}

questions.question: Which of the following sets of quantum numbers represent the highest stability of an atom?
questions.topicName: Stability of half-filled and completely filled orbitals
questions.explanation: The lower the value of (n + l) for an orbital, the lower is its energy. If two orbitals have the same (n + l) value, the orbital with lower value of n has the lower energy i.e., highest stability. Set [d] has the lower value of n, thus has the highest stability.
